<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<DIV align="left">
<A name="101"></A>
</DIV>

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>PART I</B>


<DIV align="left">
<A name="102"></A>
</DIV>

<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><B>ITEM 1. BUSINESS.</B>

<DIV align="left">
<A name="103"></A>
</DIV>

<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt">GENERAL


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Airgas, Inc. and subsidiaries (&#147;Airgas&#148; or the &#147;Company&#148;) is the largest
U.S. distributor of industrial, medical and specialty gases (delivered in
&#147;packaged&#148; or cylinder form), and welding, safety and related products
(&#147;hardgoods&#148;). Airgas also produces dry ice, liquid carbon dioxide, nitrous
oxide, process chemicals and specialty gases for distribution throughout the
United States. Airgas&#146; integrated network of over 800 locations includes
branches, retail stores, packaged gas fill plants, specialty gas labs,
production facilities and distribution centers. Airgas also distributes its
products and services to its diversified customer base through e-Business,
catalog and telesales channels. Sales were $1.90&nbsp;billion, $1.79&nbsp;billion, and
$1.64&nbsp;billion in fiscal years 2004, 2003, and 2002, respectively.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;At December&nbsp;31, 2003, the Company elected to adopt Financial Accounting
Standards Board Interpretation No.&nbsp;46R, <I>Consolidation of Variable Interest
Entities, </I>(&#147;FIN 46R&#148;) with respect to its joint venture, National Welders
Supply Company, Inc. (&#147;National Welders&#148;) and consolidated this formerly
unconsolidated affiliate. Since June&nbsp;1996, the Company has participated in the
joint venture and has used the equity method of accounting to report the
Company&#146;s proportionate share of the joint venture&#146;s operating results.
Consequently, the consolidation of National Welders had no impact on the net
earnings of the Company. In addition, the consolidation did not change the
fact that the liabilities of the joint venture are non-recourse to the Company
and the cash flows, in excess of a management fee paid by National Welders, are
not available to the Company. Through the nine months ended December&nbsp;31, 2003,
the Company recognized $4.4&nbsp;million of &#147;Equity in Earnings of Unconsolidated
Affiliates&#148; related to National Welders. With the prospective adoption
permitted by FIN 46R (prior periods not restated), the fourth fiscal quarter
operating results of the joint venture were reflected broadly across the income
statement with minority interest expense reflecting the preferred stockholders&#146;
proportionate share of the joint venture&#146;s operating results. For the fourth
quarter of fiscal 2004, National Welders contributed $39.2&nbsp;million to sales,
$3.4&nbsp;million to operating income, $291 thousand of minority interest expense
and $1.2&nbsp;million in net earnings.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company has two operating segments, Distribution and All Other
Operations. For the purpose of disclosing operating results by business
segment, the Company renamed its Gas Operations segment as &#147;All Other
Operations&#148; and disclosed the operating results of National Welders with the
results of business units previously reported under that segment. These
business units produce and distribute dry ice, carbon dioxide, nitrous oxide
and specialty gases. The operating results of the Company&#146;s two air separation
plants and its six national specialty gas labs are also reported under this
segment. The National Welders joint venture structure, which limits the
Company&#146;s control over the National Welders operations and cash flows, is the
primary factor that led the Company to conclude that National Welders is most
appropriately reflected in the All Other Operations segment. The business
units reflected in the All Other Operations segment individually do not meet
the materiality thresholds to be reported as separate business segments.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;On April&nbsp;2, 2004, the Company announced that it signed a definitive asset
purchase agreement to acquire most of the U.S. packaged gas business of The BOC
Group, Inc. (&#147;BOC&#148;) in a transaction valued up to $200&nbsp;million. The
transaction is expected to close July&nbsp;30, 2004 and includes approximately 120
locations in 21 states involved in the distribution of packaged industrial,
medical and specialty gases, as well as welding hardgoods. In fiscal 2003, the
packaged gas business of BOC, subject to the acquisition, generated
approximately $240&nbsp;million in sales of which approximately 65% was gas and
rent. The acquired operations will be integrated with the existing business
units of the Company&#146;s Distribution segment.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">3
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Financial information by business segment can be found in Item&nbsp;7,
&#147;Management&#146;s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of
Operations&#148; (&#147;MD&#038;A&#148;), and in Note 24 to the Company&#146;s Consolidated Financial
Statements under Item&nbsp;8, &#147;Financial Statements and Supplementary Data.&#148;
Descriptions of the operating segments are as follows:

<DIV align="left">
<A name="104"></A>
</DIV>

<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt">DISTRIBUTION


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Distribution segment accounts for approximately 90% of consolidated
sales and reflects the distribution of industrial, medical and specialty gases,
process chemicals and hardgoods.


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Principal Products and Services</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Distribution segment&#146;s principal products and services include
packaged and small bulk gases, gas cylinder and welding equipment rental,
process chemicals and hardgoods. Gas sales include industrial, medical and
specialty gases such as: nitrogen, oxygen, argon, helium, acetylene, carbon
dioxide, nitrous oxide, hydrogen, welding gases, ultra high purity grades and
special application blends. Rent is derived from gas cylinders, cryogenic
liquid containers, bulk storage tanks, tube trailers and through the rental of
welding equipment. Gas and rent represented approximately 52%, 53%, and 47% of
the Distribution segment&#146;s sales in each of the fiscal years 2004, 2003 and
2002, respectively. Hardgoods consist of welding supplies and equipment,
safety products, and industrial tools and supplies. In each of the fiscal
years 2004, 2003, and 2002, hardgoods sales represented approximately 48%, 47%,
and 53% of the Distribution segment&#146;s sales, respectively (see Note 24 of the
Company&#146;s Consolidated Financial Statements for additional information
regarding segment sales).


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Principal Markets and Methods of Distribution</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company believes that the market for industrial, medical and specialty
gases in the United States is approximately $9.4&nbsp;billion annually. The
industry has three principal modes of distribution: on-site supply, bulk or
merchant supply, and cylinder  supply. In the U.S. market,
on-site supply accounts for approximately 25% of sales, bulk or merchant supply
accounts for approximately 35% of sales, and cylinder supply accounts for
the remaining 40% or $3.8&nbsp;billion in sales. Airgas&#146; market focus has been on
the packaged gas segment of the market, which generally consists of customers who purchase gases in
cylinders and in less than truck load bulk quantities. Generally, packaged gas distributors also distribute
welding products. The Company believes the U.S. market for welding products to
be approximately $4.0&nbsp;billion annually.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Airgas is the largest distributor of packaged gases and welding products
in the United States, with approximately a 19% market share. The Company&#146;s
competitors in this market are approximately 900 independent distributors that
serve approximately 50% of the market through a fragmented distribution
network. Large distributors, including vertically integrated gas producers
such as Praxair, Inc. (&#147;Praxair&#148;), Liquid Air Corporation of America (&#147;Air
Liquide&#148;), and BOC, serve the remaining 31% of the packaged gas market. The
acquisition of the BOC packaged gas business described above will not affect
BOC&#146;s on-site and most of its bulk supply business in the U.S. The Company
also sells safety equipment. The United States market for safety equipment is
approximately $6&nbsp;billion, of which Airgas&#146; share is approximately 4%.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">4
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company&#146;s customer base is broad and includes many major industries.
The Company estimates the following industry segments account for the indicated
percentages of the Company&#146;s total sales:

<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Suppliers</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company purchases industrial, medical and specialty gases pursuant to
requirements contracts from national and regional producers of industrial
gases. In February&nbsp;2002, the Company entered into a 15-year take-or-pay supply
agreement under which Air Products and Chemicals, Inc. (&#147;Air Products&#148;)
supplies at least 35% of the Company&#146;s bulk liquid nitrogen, oxygen and argon
requirements, exclusive of the volumes purchased under the BOC supply agreement
noted below. Additionally, the Company purchases helium from Air Products
under the terms of the supply agreement. Effective December&nbsp;1, 2002, the
Company entered into a 3-year take-or-pay supply agreement with BOC to purchase
liquid nitrogen, oxygen and argon. With the BOC acquisition, both the Company
and BOC will also sign new reciprocal long-term supply agreements. The Company
will become the supplier for a substantial portion of BOC&#146;s resale packaged gas
needs. BOC will supply the Company with approximately 85% of the liquid bulk
gases volumes transferred to the Company in the acquisition. Both the Air
Products and BOC supply agreements contain market pricing subject to certain
economic indices and market analysis. Furthermore, the Company believes the
minimum product purchases under the agreements are well within the Company&#146;s
normal product purchases.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company also manufactures certain gases, including acetylene, nitrous
oxide, nitrogen, oxygen and argon. The Company believes that, if a contractual
arrangement with any supplier of gases or other raw materials was terminated,
it would be able to locate alternative sources of supply without disruption of
service. The Company purchases hardgoods from major manufacturers and
suppliers. For certain products, the Company has negotiated national
purchasing arrangements. The Company believes that if an arrangement with any
supplier of hardgoods were terminated, it would be able to arrange comparable
alternative supply arrangements.

<DIV align="left">
<A name="105"></A>
</DIV>

<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt">ALL OTHER OPERATIONS


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The All Other Operations segment produces and distributes certain gas
products, principally dry ice, carbon dioxide, nitrous oxide and specialty
gases. The Company also operates five air separation plants that produce
oxygen, nitrogen and argon which are sold to on-site customers and to the
Distribution segment. A description of the businesses included in the All
Other Operations segment follows:


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">5
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Dry Ice</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company is a producer and distributor of dry ice. With 14 dry ice
plants (converting liquid carbon dioxide into dry ice), the Company has the
largest network of conversion plants in the United States. Customers include
food processors, food service, pharmaceutical and biotech industries, wholesale
trade and grocery and other retail outlets. The dry ice business generally
experiences a higher level of sales during the warmer months. The Company&#146;s
carbon dioxide requirements are purchased from the vertically integrated
producers of carbon dioxide and from internal production sources.


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Carbon Dioxide</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company is a producer and distributor of liquid carbon dioxide.
Carbon dioxide is a byproduct of other chemical processes, typically the
production of ammonia or ethanol. Carbon dioxide can also be extracted from
natural wells. The Company&#146;s requirements are met by nine Company-owned
production facilities including a newly constructed plant in Hopewell VA, a
50%-owned joint venture, and long-term supply contracts with vertically
integrated gas producers. The Company believes the United States bulk supply
market for liquid carbon dioxide is approximately $500&nbsp;million annually. The
largest customer segments include chemical producers and manufacturers of foods
and beverages. The Company&#146;s market share is approximately 14%, making it the
third largest marketer of liquid carbon dioxide in the United States. However,
the Company is heavily concentrated in the southeastern United States and
maintains a 36% market share in that region.


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Specialty and Other Gases</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company operates six national specialty gas labs and a specialty gas
equipment center. The national specialty gas labs are full-scale testing and
blending facilities. They blend various special application gas mixtures,
ultra high purity grade gases, multi-component hydrocarbon blended EPA protocol
gases, and vehicle emission standard gases. Gas mixtures are used in process
control, final product qualification and emissions monitoring. Specialty gases
and specialty gas equipment produced by these facilities are primarily sold to
the Distribution segment (see Note 24 of the Company&#146;s Consolidated Financial
Statements for disclosure related to inter-segment sales). The third-party
customer base for these products consists primarily of governmental entities
and environmental-related businesses, manufacturers of electronics, petroleum
refiners, and pharmaceutical companies. These operations also provide quality
management and technical support to 45 regional labs, which are operated by the
Distribution segment and one lab operated by National Welders. The national
and regional labs perform testing and certification services for gas purity.
Nearly half of the Company&#146;s labs are certified to the ISO 9001:2000 standard
for quality management in the manufacture and sale of specialty gases.


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Nitrous Oxide</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company is a manufacturer of nitrous oxide gas. Nitrous oxide is used
as an anesthetic in the medical and dental fields, as a propellant in the
packaged food business and is utilized in the manufacturing process of certain
electronics industries. The Company&#146;s market focus includes bulk customers as
well as sales to the Distribution segment. The Company purchases the raw
materials utilized in its nitrous oxide production pursuant to contracts with
major manufacturers and suppliers.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">6
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>National Welders Supply Company, Inc.</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Effective December&nbsp;31, 2003 as a result of adopting FIN 46R, the Company
consolidated National Welders, whose results through December&nbsp;31, 2003 were
reflected as equity in the earnings of unconsolidated affiliates below
operating income. National Welders is a producer and distributor of industrial
gases based in Charlotte, North Carolina. The joint venture has significant
production capabilities with its three air separation plants, two acetylene
plants and a specialty gas lab. It also distributes packaged gases and welding
products through its 46 branch stores. The ownership interests in the joint
venture consist of voting common stock and voting, redeemable preferred stock.
The Company owns 100% of the joint venture&#146;s common stock, which represents a
50% voting interest. The Company does not hold a majority voting interest in
the joint venture and, therefore, historically used the equity method to
account for its interest in the joint venture. In fiscal 2005, the
consolidation of National Welders is expected to contribute approximately $100
million in additional revenue. However, the new presentation is not expected
to have a material impact on earnings.


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Suppliers</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company believes that if a contractual arrangement with any All Other
Operations segment supplier was terminated, it would not have a material
adverse effect on operations. However, two of the Company&#146;s 14 dry ice
production facilities are located on property owned by BOC. If the current
arrangements with BOC were terminated, the Company&#146;s dry ice production
capabilities may be reduced.

<DIV align="left">
<A name="106"></A>
</DIV>

<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt">AIRGAS GROWTH STRATEGIES


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company&#146;s primary objective is to maximize shareholder value by
driving market-leading sales growth through leveraging its national
distribution infrastructure. To meet this objective, the Company is focusing
on:


<P>
<TABLE width="100%" border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" style="font-size: 10pt">

<TR valign="top" style="font-size: 10pt; color: #000000; background: transparent">
    <TD width="1%" nowrap align="right">&#149;</TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>Customer service strategies focused on growing our business
with small and medium-sized core customers;</TD>
</TR>

<TR>
    <TD colspan="5">&nbsp;</TD>
</TR>

<TR valign="top" style="font-size: 10pt; color: #000000; background: transparent">
    <TD width="1%" nowrap align="right">&#149;</TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>A new medical strategy with the formation of the Airgas Puritan Medical division within each regional distribution company
offering an integrated portfolio of bulk and cylinder gases, medical equipment and services to hospitals, home healthcare
providers and offices of medical professionals;</TD>
</TR>

<TR>
    <TD colspan="5">&nbsp;</TD>
</TR>

<TR valign="top" style="font-size: 10pt; color: #000000; background: transparent">
    <TD width="1%" nowrap align="right">&#149;</TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>Opportunities in niche markets including bulk, medical and specialty gases, as well as safety products;</TD>
</TR>

<TR>
    <TD colspan="5">&nbsp;</TD>

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;These strategic objectives are primarily designed to create a strong and
stable platform to drive market-leading sales growth.

<DIV align="left">
<A name="107"></A>
</DIV>

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">REGULATORY AND ENVIRONMENTAL MATTERS



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company&#146;s subsidiaries are subject to federal and state laws and
regulations adopted for the protection of the environment and the health and
safety of employees and users of the Company&#146;s products. The Company has
programs for the operation and design of its facilities to achieve compliance
with applicable environmental regulations. The Company believes that it is in
compliance, in all-material respects, with such laws and regulations.
Expenditures for environmental compliance purposes during fiscal 2004 were not
material.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">7
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">
<DIV align="left">
<A name="108"></A>
</DIV>

<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt">INSURANCE


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company has established insurance programs to cover workers&#146;
compensation, business automobile, general and product liability
claims. These
programs have self-insured retention of $500 thousand per occurrence and an
additional annual aggregate limit of $1.7&nbsp;million of claims in excess of $500
thousand. The Company accrues estimated losses using actuarial models and
assumptions based on the Company&#146;s historical loss experience. National
Welders is also self-insured for workers&#146; compensation claims. Workers&#146;
compensation claims are self-insured up to $250 thousand per person annually
with an aggregate liability of $575 thousand. Provisions for expected future
claim payments are accrued based on estimates of the aggregate liability for
claims incurred plus an estimate for incurred but not reported claims using
historical experience.

<DIV align="left">
<A name="109"></A>
</DIV>

<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt">EMPLOYEES


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;On March&nbsp;31, 2004, the Company employed approximately 9,700 employees
(including 800 employees employed by National Welders) of whom less than 5%
were covered by collective bargaining agreements. Through the fiscal 2005
acquisition of the U.S. packaged gas operations of BOC, the Company intends to
offer employment to more than 1,000 BOC employees aligned with those
operations. The Company believes it has good relations with its employees and
has not experienced a significant strike or work stoppage in over ten years.

<DIV align="left">
<A name="110"></A>
</DIV>

&#147;Red-D-Arc,&#148; &#147;RED-D-ARC WELDERENTAL,&#148; &#147;Gold Gas,&#148; &#147;SteelMIX,&#148; &#147;Outlook,&#148;
&#147;Ny-Trous&#043;,&#148; &#147;Powersource,&#148; and &#147;RADNOR&#148; branded products. The Company also
holds trademarks for &#147;AluMIX,&#148; &#147;StainMIX,&#148; &#147;Freshblend,&#148; &#147;Walk-O2-Bout,&#148;
&#147;AcuGrav,&#148; &#147;SightSense,&#148; and &#147;SoundSense.&#148; The Company believes that its
businesses as a whole are not materially dependent upon any single patent,
trademark or license.

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">




<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;McCausland has been Chairman of the Board and Chief Executive Officer
of the Company since May&nbsp;1987. Mr.&nbsp;McCausland has also served as President
from June&nbsp;1986 to August&nbsp;1988, from April&nbsp;1993 to November&nbsp;1995, and from April
1997 to January&nbsp;1999. Mr.&nbsp;McCausland also serves as a director of the Fox
Chase Cancer Center, the Independence Seaport Museum, the International
Oxygen Manufacturers Association, Inc. and
the Eisenhower Exchange Fellowships, Inc.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Fischer has been President and Chief Operating Officer since November
2000. Prior to joining Airgas, Mr.&nbsp;Fischer served as President of BOC Gases -
North America from 1997 to 2000 and as Executive Vice President of BOC Gases -
Americas from 1995 to 1997.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Millay has been Senior Vice President and Chief Financial Officer
since November&nbsp;1999. Prior to joining Airgas, Mr.&nbsp;Millay served as Senior Vice
President and Chief Financial Officer of Transport International Pool, a
division of General Electric Capital Corporation, from May&nbsp;1995 to October
1999.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Dougherty has been Senior Vice President and Chief Information Officer
since joining Airgas in January&nbsp;2001. Prior to joining Airgas, Mr.&nbsp;Dougherty
served as Vice President and Chief Information Officer from August&nbsp;1998 to
December&nbsp;2000 and as Director of Information Systems from November&nbsp;1993 to July
1998 of Subaru of America, Inc.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Keen has been Senior Vice President &#151; Law and Corporate Development
since April&nbsp;1997. Prior to that time, Mr.&nbsp;Keen served as Vice President -
Corporate Development from January&nbsp;1992 to March&nbsp;1997.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Molinini has been Senior Vice President &#151; Hardgoods since August&nbsp;2000.
Prior to that time, Mr.&nbsp;Molinini served as Vice President &#151; Hardgoods
Operations from August&nbsp;1999 to July&nbsp;2000 and as Vice President &#151; Airgas Direct
Industrial from April&nbsp;1997 to July&nbsp;1999. Prior to joining Airgas, Mr.&nbsp;Molinini
served as Vice President of Marketing of National Welders Supply Company, Inc.
since 1991.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Visintainer has been Senior Vice President &#151; Sales since January&nbsp;1999.
Prior to that time, Mr.&nbsp;Visintainer served as Vice President &#151; Sales and
Marketing from February&nbsp;1998 to December&nbsp;1998 and as President of one of the
Company&#146;s subsidiaries from April&nbsp;1996 to January&nbsp;1998. Until March&nbsp;1996, he
was employed by BOC Gases and served in various field positions including
National Sales Manager &#151; Industrial/Specialty Gases and National Accounts
Manager.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Wilson was appointed Senior Vice President &#151; Human Resources in
January&nbsp;2004. Prior to joining Airgas, Mr.&nbsp;Wilson served as Senior Vice
President, Corporate Resources at DecisionOne Corporation from October&nbsp;1995 to
December&nbsp;2003.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Crichton has been Division President &#151; West since February&nbsp;1993.
Prior to that time, Mr.&nbsp;Crichton served in various leadership positions since
joining the Company in 1988 and has more than 30&nbsp;years of experience in the
industrial gas industry.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Powers has been Division President &#151; East since joining Airgas in
April&nbsp;2001. Prior to joining Airgas, Mr.&nbsp;Powers served as Senior Vice
President of Industrial Gases at AGA from October&nbsp;1995 to March&nbsp;2001. Mr.
Powers has more than 25 years of experience in the industrial gas industry.

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;Schulte has been Division President &#150; Gas Operations since February
2003. Prior to that time, Mr.&nbsp;Schulte served as Senior Vice President &#150; Gas
Operations from August&nbsp;2000 to January&nbsp;2003, as Vice President &#150; Gas Operations
from November&nbsp;1998 to July&nbsp;2000 and as President of Airgas Carbonic from

Sales of Arcadian Corporation from 1992 through June&nbsp;1997.

and as an Associate with the law firm of Brown &#038; Wood, LLP from 1994 to 1999.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Mr.&nbsp;McLaughlin has been Vice President and Controller since joining Airgas

Finance for Asbury Automotive Group from 1999 to 2001, and was a Vice President
and held various senior financial positions at Unisource Worldwide,
Inc. from 1992 to 1999.


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt">COMPANY INFORMATION


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company&#146;s Annual Report on Form 10-K, Quarterly Reports on Form 10-Q,
Current Reports on Form 8-K and any amendments to those reports filed with or
furnished to the Securities and Exchange Commission (&#147;SEC&#148;) are available free
of charge on the Company&#146;s website (www.airgas.com) under the &#147;Investors&#148; section. The
Company makes these documents available as soon as reasonably practicable after
they are filed with or furnished to the SEC, but no later than the end of the
day in which they are filed or furnished to the SEC.

<DIV align="left">
<A name="112"></A>
</DIV>

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>ITEM 2. PROPERTIES.</B>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The principal executive offices of the Company are located in leased space
in Radnor, Pennsylvania.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company&#146;s Distribution segment operates a network of multiple use
facilities consisting of approximately 600 branch stores, 45 regional gas
laboratories, 18 acetylene manufacturing facilities, 5 regional distribution
centers, 200 cylinder fill plants and various customer call centers. The
Distribution segment conducts business in 46 states. The Company owns
approximately 28% of these facilities. The remaining facilities are primarily
leased from third parties. A limited number of facilities are leased from
employees and are on terms consistent with commercial rental rates prevailing
in the surrounding rental market.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company&#146;s All Other Operations segment consists of businesses, located
throughout the United States, which operate multiple use facilities consisting
of 86 branch locations, 11 liquid carbon dioxide and 14 dry ice production
facilities, 5 air separation plants, 6 national specialty gas laboratories, and 4 nitrous
oxide production facilities. The Company owns approximately 36% of these
facilities. The remaining facilities are leased from third parties.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;During fiscal 2004, the Company&#146;s production facilities operated at
approximately 79% of capacity based on an average daily production shift of 14
hours. If required, additional shifts could be run to expand production
capacity.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company believes that its facilities are adequate for its present
needs and that its properties are generally in good condition, well maintained
and suitable for their intended use.

<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><B>ITEM 3. LEGAL PROCEEDINGS.</B>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company is involved in various legal and regulatory proceedings that
have arisen in the ordinary course of its business and have not been fully
adjudicated. These actions, when ultimately concluded and determined, will
not, in the opinion of management, have a material adverse effect upon the
Company&#146;s consolidated financial condition, results of operations or liquidity.

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The closing sale price of the Company&#146;s common stock as reported by the
New York Stock Exchange on June&nbsp;9, 2004, was $22.69 per share.
As of June 9,
2004, there were approximately 14,000 stockholders of record of the Company&#146;s
common stock.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;On May&nbsp;13, 2003, the Company&#146;s Board of Directors declared the first
quarterly cash dividend in the Company&#146;s history of $.04 per share, which was
paid to stockholders on June&nbsp;30, 2003.  At the end of each
quarter during fiscal 2004, the Company paid its stockholders regular quarterly
cash dividends of $0.04 per share. In addition, on May&nbsp;25, 2004, the Company&#146;s
Board of Directors declared a regular quarterly cash dividend of $0.045 per
share payable June&nbsp;30, 2004 to stockholders of record as of June&nbsp;15, 2004.
Future dividend declarations and associated amounts paid will depend upon the
Company&#146;s earnings, financial condition, loan covenants, capital requirements
and other factors deemed relevant by management and the Company&#146;s Board of
Directors.

    <TD width="96%">As discussed in &#147;Item&nbsp;7. Management&#146;s Discussion and Analysis of
Financial Condition and Results of Operations&#148; and in the notes to the
Company&#146;s Consolidated Financial Statements included in Item&nbsp;8, the
results for fiscal 2004 include a fourth quarter special charge recovery
of $776 thousand ($480 thousand after tax) reflecting lower estimates of
the ultimate cost of prior years&#146; restructuring activities. Fiscal 2004
results also include the fourth quarter consolidation of the National
Welders joint venture in accordance with FIN 46R. National Welders
contributed $39.2&nbsp;million of sales and $3.4&nbsp;million of operating income in
fiscal 2004. As of March&nbsp;31, 2004, National Welders also contributed
current assets of $30&nbsp;million, total assets of $140&nbsp;million, current
liabilities of $21&nbsp;million, and non-current liabilities of $119&nbsp;million
(including a minority interest liability of $36&nbsp;million).</TD>

was paid to stockholders on June&nbsp;30, 2003. At the end of
each quarter during fiscal 2004, the Company paid its stockholders regular
quarterly cash dividends of $0.04 per share. In addition, on May&nbsp;25,
2004, the Company&#146;s Board of Directors declared a regular quarterly cash
dividend of $0.045 per share payable June&nbsp;30, 2004 to stockholders of
record as of June&nbsp;15, 2004. Future dividend declarations and associated
amounts paid will depend upon the Company&#146;s earnings, financial condition,
loan covenants, capital requirements and other factors deemed relevant by
management and the Company&#146;s Board of Directors.</TD>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">






<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>AIRGAS, INC. AND
SUBSIDIARIES</B>

<DIV align="left">
<A name="118"></A>
</DIV>

<DIV align="center" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>MANAGEMENT&#146;S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS<BR>
OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS</B></DIV>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>Item&nbsp;7.</B>



<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><B>RESULTS OF OPERATIONS: 2004 COMPARED TO 2003</B>


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><B>OVERVIEW</B>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company&#146;s net sales for the fiscal year ended March&nbsp;31, 2004
(&#147;fiscal 2004&#148;) were $1.90&nbsp;billion compared to $1.79&nbsp;billion in the prior
year (&#147;fiscal 2003&#148;). Sales growth of $109&nbsp;million was driven by
acquisitions, same-store sales growth and the December&nbsp;31, 2003
consolidation of National Welders. During fiscal 2004, the Company
completed five acquisitions with combined annual sales of approximately
$59&nbsp;million. Strategic product sales initiatives related to medical, bulk
and specialty gases, and strategic accounts, contributed to a 1% increase
in total same-store sales. Sales growth was achieved despite weakness in the
manufacturing and industrial markets during much of fiscal 2004. While
same-store sales were flat through the first nine months of fiscal 2004,
they began rebounding in the fourth quarter led by sales of hardgoods,
which increased significantly (9%) as the industrial marketplace began to
show signs of an economic recovery. Gas and rent sales also showed
improvement (2%) in the fiscal fourth quarter. Over the past several
years, the Company has embarked on several programs to strengthen its
infrastructure in anticipation of continued growth. As a result,
management believes that the Company is well positioned to take advantage
of a rebounding industrial economy.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Fiscal 2004 net earnings were $80.2&nbsp;million, or $1.07 per diluted share,
compared to $68.1&nbsp;million, or $0.94 per diluted share, in fiscal 2003.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;As discussed in the &#147;Income Statement Commentary&#148; below, fiscal 2004
results were affected by the following:

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Fiscal 2003 results were affected by the following:


<P>
<TABLE width="100%" border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" style="font-size: 10pt">

    <TD width="1%" nowrap align="right">&#149;</TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>special and other charges totaling $2.9&nbsp;million ($2.2&nbsp;million after-tax),
or $0.03 per diluted share, primarily consisting of a restructuring charge
($2.7&nbsp;million) related to the integration of the business acquired from
Air Products and Chemicals, Inc. (&#147;Air Products&#148;) in fiscal 2002 and costs
related to the consolidation of certain hardgoods procurement functions.</TD>
</TR>

</TABLE>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;On April&nbsp;2, 2004, the Company announced that it signed a definitive asset
purchase agreement to acquire most of the U.S. packaged gas business of The BOC
Group, Inc. (&#147;BOC&#148;) in a transaction valued up to $200&nbsp;million. The
transaction is expected to close July&nbsp;30, 2004 and includes approximately 120
locations in 21 states involved in the distribution of packaged industrial,
medical and specialty gases, as well as welding hardgoods. In fiscal 2003, the
packaged gas business of BOC, subject to the acquisition, generated
approximately $240&nbsp;million in sales of which approximately 65% was gas and
rent. The acquired operations will be integrated with the existing business
units of the Company&#146;s Distribution segment.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">14
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;At December&nbsp;31, 2003, the Company elected to adopt Financial Accounting
Standards Board Interpretation No.&nbsp;46R, <I>Consolidation of Variable Interest
Entities, </I>(&#147;FIN 46R&#148;) with respect to its joint venture, National Welders
Supply Company, Inc. (&#147;National Welders&#148;) and consolidated this formerly
unconsolidated affiliate. Since June&nbsp;1996, the Company has participated in the
joint venture and has used the equity method of accounting to report the
Company&#146;s proportionate share of the joint venture&#146;s operating results.
Consequently, the consolidation of National Welders had no impact on the net
earnings of the Company. In addition, the consolidation did not change the
fact that the liabilities of the joint venture are non-recourse to the Company
and the cash flows, in excess of a management fee paid by National Welders, are
not available to the Company. Through the nine months ended December&nbsp;31, 2003,
the Company recognized $4.4&nbsp;million of &#147;Equity in Earnings of Unconsolidated
Affiliates&#148; related to National Welders. With the prospective adoption
permitted by FIN 46R (prior periods not restated), the fourth fiscal quarter
operating results of the joint venture were reflected broadly across the income
statement with minority interest expense reflecting the preferred stockholders&#146;
proportionate share of the joint venture&#146;s operating results. For the fourth
quarter of fiscal 2004, National Welders contributed $39.2&nbsp;million to sales,
$3.4&nbsp;million to operating income, $291 thousand of minority interest expense
and $1.2&nbsp;million in net earnings.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company generated significant cash flow in fiscal 2004, which enabled
the Company to finance acquisitions and capital expenditures and to repay debt.
In addition, the Company&#146;s strong cash flow fundamentals enabled it to pay its
first quarterly cash dividend in the Company&#146;s history of $0.04 per share on
June&nbsp;30, 2003. Subsequently, at the end of each quarter during fiscal 2004,
the Company paid its stockholders regular quarterly cash dividends of $0.04 per
share. In addition, on May&nbsp;25, 2004, the Company announced that its Board of
Directors declared a regular quarterly cash dividend of $0.045 per share,
representing a 13% increase compared to the prior year quarterly dividend. The
dividend is payable June&nbsp;30, 2004 to stockholders of record as of June&nbsp;15,
2004.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Looking forward, fiscal 2005 appears to hold significant opportunities for
the Company. The BOC acquisition will be the largest acquisition in the
Company&#146;s history. The process of integrating BOC&#146;s packaged gas business into
the Company&#146;s distribution network will provide both challenges and
opportunities. The Company anticipates that the economic recovery will continue
during fiscal 2005. Given these factors, the Company estimates that fiscal
2005 net earnings will be approximately $1.21 to $1.27 per diluted share, which
includes up to $0.02 per diluted share accretion from the BOC acquisition.
Additionally, in the first quarter of fiscal 2005, the Company estimates that
it will earn $0.27 to $0.31 per diluted share, which includes modest dilution
from pre-integration expenses associated with the BOC acquisition. The fiscal
2005 range of estimated net earnings anticipates a continued supportive sales
environment with gas and rent sales rising throughout the year. Actual fiscal
2005 net earnings may be impacted by a number of factors including the pace of
the economic recovery, the sales mix of gas and rent versus hardgoods, the
interest rate environment, and the costs to integrate the BOC acquisition.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">15

<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><B>INCOME STATEMENT COMMENTARY</B>


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Net Sales</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Net sales increased 6% in fiscal 2004 compared to fiscal 2003 driven
primarily by acquisitions, same-store sales growth of 1% and the consolidation
of National Welders. The Company estimates same-store sales based on a
comparison of current period sales to prior period sales, adjusted for
acquisitions and divestitures. The pro-forma adjustments consist of adding
acquired sales to, or subtracting sales of divested operations from, sales
reported in the prior period. These pro-forma adjustments used in calculating
the same-store sales metric are not reflected in the table below. The
intercompany eliminations represent sales from All Other Operations to the
Distribution segment.

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Distribution segment&#146;s principal products include industrial, medical
and specialty gases; process chemicals; equipment rental and hardgoods.
Industrial, medical and specialty gases and process chemicals are distributed
in cylinders or bulk containers. Equipment rental fees are generally charged
on cylinders, cryogenic liquid containers, bulk and micro-bulk tanks, tube
trailers and welding equipment. Hardgoods consist of welding supplies and
equipment, safety products, and industrial tools and supplies.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Distribution segment sales increased $60.4&nbsp;million (4%) driven by
acquisitions and growth in same-store sales. The Company estimates that
business acquisitions contributed $39.3&nbsp;million to sales in fiscal 2004.
Same-store sales growth of $21.1&nbsp;million (1%) resulted from increases in
hardgoods sales of $12.9&nbsp;million (1%) and gas and rent sales of $8.2&nbsp;million
(1%). Through the first nine months of fiscal 2004, Distribution same-store
sales were negative, driven by lower sales of hardgoods reflecting weakness in
the industrial and manufacturing sectors of the economy. However, same-store
sales of hardgoods increased significantly (9%) in the fiscal fourth quarter as
the industrial marketplace began to show signs of an economic recovery. Gas
and rent sales also showed improvement (2%) in the fiscal fourth quarter.
Same-store sales of safety products increased a strong 6% during fiscal 2004 as
the Company continued its cross-selling strategy of marketing safety products
to its broad base of customers. In addition, safety sales were helped by a new
channel migration program that utilizes telemarketing to sell safety products
to several thousand low volume customers.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company has followed a strategy of focusing on opportunities in niche
markets to facilitate sales growth. The strategy focuses on products and
markets that are expected to grow at a faster rate than the overall economy.
These growth initiatives relate to sales of strategic products, including
medical and specialty gases, gases sold in bulk and micro-bulk containers,
safety products, and sales to strategic account customers. During fiscal 2004,
these sales initiatives contributed to sales growth of gas and rent, which
helped mitigate weakness in the industrial economy. Medical gas and rent
revenues increased 8% to $146&nbsp;million versus the prior year reflecting volume
gains. Sales growth of specialty gases fell short of expectations, driven by
pricing pressure and sluggish demand associated with economic conditions. Bulk
gas and rent revenues grew 8% to $107&nbsp;million reflecting higher volumes as well
as the promotion of the Company&#146;s micro-bulk program. The micro-bulk program
is designed to service niche customers who require higher volumes of gases than
can be provided efficiently through the use of gases packaged in cylinders.
Sales to strategic account customers (sales to large customers with multiple
locations) increased 3% to $255&nbsp;million in fiscal 2004. Rental revenue was
also favorably impacted by a 7% increase in welding equipment rentals and sales
associated with the Company&#146;s continued expansion of its rental welder fleet.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">16
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The All Other Operations segment consists of producers and distributors of
gas products, principally of dry ice, carbon dioxide, nitrous oxide and
specialty gases. The segment also includes the Company&#146;s National Welders
joint venture, which was consolidated effective December&nbsp;31, 2003. All Other
Operations&#146; sales, net of intercompany sales eliminations,
increased $48.1 million (33%), principally from the consolidation of National Welders,
same-store sales growth and an acquisition during fiscal 2004. National
Welders contributed sales of $39.2&nbsp;million in the fourth quarter and fiscal
year. Had National Welders been consolidated for all of fiscal 2004, it would
have contributed sales of $147&nbsp;million. Same-store sales growth of 5% was
principally the result of higher sales volumes of liquid carbon dioxide
reflecting the additional source of product from the Hopewell, Virginia plant
that began operations in January&nbsp;2003. Same-store sales growth associated with
liquid carbon dioxide was partially offset by lower sales of dry ice. Sales of
dry ice were negatively impacted during fiscal 2004 by significant pricing
pressure related to additional competition in the market. The acquisition of a
dry ice business in April&nbsp;2003 also contributed sales of $2.2&nbsp;million in fiscal
2004.


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Gross Profits</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Gross profits do not reflect depreciation expense and distribution costs.
As disclosed in Note 1 to the Consolidated Financial Statements, the Company
reflects distribution costs as elements of Selling, Distribution and
Administrative Expenses and recognizes depreciation on all its property, plant
and equipment on the income statement line item &#147;Depreciation.&#148; Some companies
may report certain or all of these costs as elements of their Cost of Products
Sold. Consequently, gross profits discussed below may not be comparable to
those of other entities.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Gross profits increased 5%, while the gross profit margin decreased 30
basis points to 52.1% in fiscal 2004 compared to 52.4% in fiscal 2003.

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Distribution segment&#146;s gross profits increased $21.3&nbsp;million (3%)
compared to the prior year. Distribution&#146;s gross profit margin of 50.3% in
fiscal 2004 decreased 60 basis points from 50.9% in fiscal 2003. The lower
gross profit margin primarily resulted from a shift in sales mix away from
higher margin gas and rent sales and towards lower margin sales of hardgoods.
Acquisitions of two distributors of safety products also contributed to the
shift in mix. The Distribution segment&#146;s sales consisted of 51.8% gas and rent
compared to 52.6% in fiscal 2003.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;All Other Operations&#146; gross profits increased $28.9&nbsp;million (29%) compared
to the prior year. Higher gross profits primarily reflect the consolidation of
National Welders, higher sales volumes of liquid carbon dioxide sourced from
the new Hopewell, Virginia plant and an acquisition. The consolidation of
National Welders contributed gross profits of $22&nbsp;million. The gross profit
margin of 55% was flat compared to 54.9% in the prior year. The fourth quarter
consolidation of National Welders had an insignificant impact on the gross
profit margin. Had National Welders been consolidated for all of fiscal 2004,
the impact on the All Other Operations segment&#146;s gross profit margin would also
not have been significant.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">17
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Operating Expenses</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Selling, distribution and administrative expenses (&#147;SD&#038;A&#148;) consist of
labor and overhead associated with the purchasing, marketing and distribution
of the Company&#146;s products, as well as costs associated with a variety of
administrative functions such as legal, treasury, accounting, tax and
facility-related expenses. SD&#038;A expenses increased $33.6&nbsp;million (5%) compared
to the prior year principally from the consolidation of National Welders,
higher distribution-related expenses, costs contributed by acquired businesses
and fire-related losses. The consolidation of National Welders contributed $16
million to the increase in SD&#038;A expenses. The increase in distribution-related
expenses of approximately $5&nbsp;million was primarily driven by higher fuel,
repair and maintenance costs. Higher fuel costs were directly related to the
increase in oil prices during fiscal 2004. Acquired businesses contributed
approximately $13&nbsp;million to the increase in SD&#038;A expenses. During fiscal
2004, the Company sustained fire-related losses at certain of its plants of
$2.8&nbsp;million, which represented the Company&#146;s self-insurance retention. The
prior year included costs of $2.7&nbsp;million associated with the integration of
the packaged gas business acquired from Air Products in February&nbsp;2002.
Acquisition integration expenses were not significant during fiscal 2004. As a
percentage of net sales, SD&#038;A expenses decreased 40 basis points to 38.7%
compared to 39.1% in the prior year.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Depreciation expense of $82.6&nbsp;million in fiscal 2004 increased $9.1
million (12%) compared to $73.5&nbsp;million in fiscal 2003. The increase reflects
depreciation on the current and prior year&#146;s capital investments in revenue
producing assets, including the Hopewell carbon dioxide plant, bulk and
micro-bulk tanks and medical cylinders. The fourth quarter consolidation of
National Welders also contributed $2.6&nbsp;million to the increase in depreciation
expense.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Amortization expense of $5.4&nbsp;million in fiscal 2004 decreased $1&nbsp;million
compared to $6.4&nbsp;million in fiscal 2003. The decrease in amortization expense
was primarily attributable to the expiration of certain non-compete agreements.


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Special Charges (Recoveries)</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In fiscal 2004, a special charge recovery of $776 thousand consisted of
lower estimates of the ultimate cost of prior years&#146; restructuring charges.
The special charge recovery was included in the Distribution segment and
represented a change in estimate related to facility exit costs.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In fiscal 2003, a special charge of $2.7&nbsp;million incurred by the
Distribution segment consisted of a restructuring charge related to the
integration of the business acquired from Air Products and costs related to the
consolidation of certain hardgoods procurement functions. The special charge
included facility exit costs associated with the closure of certain facilities
and employee severance. The facilities exited and the affected employees were
part of the Company&#146;s existing operations prior to the acquisition of the Air
Products business.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>Operating Income</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Operating income increased 8% in fiscal 2004 compared to fiscal 2003. The
operating income margin increased 20 basis points to 8.9% from 8.7% in the
prior year.

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Distribution segment&#146;s operating income margin of 8% in fiscal 2004
was consistent with 7.9% in fiscal 2003. The fiscal 2004 operating income
margin reflects the lower gross profit margin, described above, effectively
offset by lower operating expenses as a percentage of sales.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The All Other Operations segment&#146;s operating income margin decreased 50
basis points to 13.3% compared to 13.8% in fiscal 2003. The decrease in the
operating income margin primarily resulted from higher operating expenses as a
percentage of sales driven by the consolidation of National Welders. National
Welders has a higher cost structure compared to the other businesses included
in the segment. Had National Welders been consolidated for all of fiscal 2004,
the operating income margin would have been 11.5%.


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Interest Expense and Discount on Securitization of Trade Receivables</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Interest expense, net, and the discount on securitization of trade
receivables totaled $45.6&nbsp;million representing a decrease of
$4.1&nbsp;million<BR>
 (-8%)
compared to the prior fiscal year. The decrease in interest expense resulted
from lower average outstanding debt levels and lower weighted-average interest
rates associated with the Company&#146;s variable rate debt. The Company&#146;s interest
expense and average outstanding debt levels were lower despite the July&nbsp;1, 2003
consolidation of a grantor trust associated with an operating lease and the
December&nbsp;31, 2003 consolidation of National Welders.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company participates in a securitization agreement with two commercial
banks to sell up to $175&nbsp;million of qualifying trade receivables. The amount
of outstanding receivables under the agreement was $162.6&nbsp;million and $158.9
million at March&nbsp;31, 2004 and 2003, respectively. Net proceeds from the sale
of trade receivables were used to reduce borrowings under the Company&#146;s
revolving credit facilities. The discount on the securitization of trade
receivables represents the difference between the carrying value of the
receivables and the proceeds from their sale. The amount of the discount
varies on a monthly basis depending on the amount of receivables sold and
market rates.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;As discussed in &#147;Liquidity and Capital Resources&#148; and in Item&nbsp;7A,
&#147;Quantitative and Qualitative Disclosures About Market Risk,&#148; the Company
manages its exposure to interest rate risk through participation in interest
rate swap agreements. Including the effect of the interest rate swap
agreements, the Company&#146;s ratio of fixed to variable interest rates at March
31, 2004 was 57% fixed to 43% variable. A majority of the Company&#146;s variable
rate debt is based on a spread over the London Interbank Offered Rate
(&#147;LIBOR&#148;). Based on the Company&#146;s outstanding variable rate debt and credit
rating at March&nbsp;31, 2004, for every 25 basis point increase in LIBOR, the
Company estimates its annual interest expense would increase approximately $900
thousand.


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Equity in Earnings of Unconsolidated Affiliates</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Equity in earnings of unconsolidated affiliates totaled $5.2&nbsp;million in
fiscal 2004 compared to $3.8&nbsp;million in the prior year. The results of
National Welders for the nine months ended December&nbsp;31, 2003 (earnings of $4.4
million) were reported in the income statement as equity in earnings of
unconsolidated affiliates. Effective January&nbsp;1, 2004, National Welders&#146;
results were reflected broadly across the income statement. The increase in
the equity in earnings of unconsolidated affiliates compared to the prior year
resulted primarily from a $1.7&nbsp;million non-recurring life insurance policy gain
recognized by National Welders in December&nbsp;2003. Prior to the date that the
Company entered into the joint venture agreement with National Welders, the
founders of National Welders had obtained life insurance policies on key
personnel in which National Welders was the named beneficiary.


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Minority Interest Expense</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Minority interest expense of $291 thousand in fiscal 2004 represents the
portion of National Welders&#146; operating results applicable to the preferred
stockholders of National Welders.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">19
</DIV>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Income
Tax Expense</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The effective income tax rate was 37.2% of pre-tax earnings in fiscal 2004 compared to 37.7% in fiscal 2003.


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>Net
Earnings </I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Net earnings in fiscal 2004 were $80.2&nbsp;million, or $1.07 per diluted share, compared to $68.1&nbsp;million, or $0.94 per diluted share, in fiscal 2003.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The weighted average number of shares outstanding used in computing earnings per diluted share was 2.4&nbsp;million shares higher in fiscal 2004 compared to fiscal 2003. The increase in the weighted average number of shares outstanding primarily resulted from stock option exercises and shares purchased by employees under the Company&#146;s Employee Stock Purchase Plans. The Company expects that the weighted average number of shares outstanding will increase 2% to 3% per year.



<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">20
</DIV>

<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>
<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>AIRGAS, INC. AND SUBSIDIARIES<BR>
MANAGEMENT&#146;S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS<BR>
OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS</B>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>RESULTS OF OPERATIONS: 2003 COMPARED TO 2002</B>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>OVERVIEW</B>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company&#146;s net sales for the fiscal year ended March&nbsp;31, 2003 (&#147;fiscal
2003&#148;) were $1.79&nbsp;billion compared to $1.64&nbsp;billion in fiscal 2002. Sales
growth was driven by the fiscal 2002 fourth quarter acquisition of the majority
of Air Products&#146; U.S. packaged gas business. In addition, the Company
continued to pursue opportunistic acquisitions to complement its national
distribution network, completing four acquisitions in fiscal 2003. Although
there was significant acquisition-related sales growth, net sales were
adversely affected by the sluggish economic environment. The weak
manufacturing and industrial markets contributed to a same-store sales decline
of 2% compared to the prior year reflecting lower hardgoods sales mitigated by
higher gas and rent sales. Higher gas and rent sales reflect the Company&#146;s
focus on strategic sales initiatives related to medical and bulk gases and
strategic account customers. The Company&#146;s sales initiatives contributed to an
8% increase in medical gas sales, a 9% increase in bulk gas delivery sales, and
a 7% increase in sales to strategic account customers. With a focus on
controlling costs and improving operational effectiveness, the Company also
embarked on other strategic initiatives, including the centralization of
certain hardgoods procurement functions, rollout of an inventory management
system, and implementation of a sales force effectiveness program.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Fiscal 2003 net earnings were $68.1&nbsp;million, or $0.94 per diluted share,
compared to a net loss of $10.4&nbsp;million, or a loss of $0.15 per diluted share,
in fiscal 2002.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;As discussed in the &#147;Income Statement Commentary&#148; below, fiscal 2003
results were affected by the following:


<P>
<TABLE width="100%" border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" style="font-size: 10pt">

<TR valign="top" style="font-size: 10pt; color: #000000; background: transparent">
    <TD width="1%" nowrap align="right">&#149;</TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>special and other charges totaling $2.9&nbsp;million ($2.2&nbsp;million after-tax), or $0.03 per diluted
share, primarily consisting of a restructuring charge ($2.7&nbsp;million) related to the integration of
the business acquired from Air Products and costs related to the consolidation of certain hardgoods
procurement functions.</TD>
</TR>

</TABLE>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Fiscal 2002 results were affected by the following:


<P>
<TABLE width="100%" border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" style="font-size: 10pt">

<TR valign="top" style="font-size: 10pt; color: #000000; background: transparent">
    <TD width="1%" nowrap align="right">&#149;</TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>a non-cash after-tax charge of $59&nbsp;million, or $0.84 per diluted share, representing the cumulative
effect of a change in accounting principle associated with the adoption of Statement of Financial
Accounting Standards No.&nbsp;142, <I>Goodwill and Other Intangible Assets</I>; and</TD>
</TR>

<TR>
    <TD colspan="5">&nbsp;</TD>
</TR>

<TR valign="top" style="font-size: 10pt; color: #000000; background: transparent">
    <TD width="1%" nowrap align="right">&#149;</TD>
    <TD width="1%">&nbsp;</TD>
    <TD>a litigation settlement charge of $8.5&nbsp;million ($5.7&nbsp;million after-tax), or $0.08 per diluted share.</TD>
</TR>

</TABLE>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;During fiscal 2003, the Company successfully integrated the business
acquired from Air Products in the prior year. In addition, during fiscal 2003,
the Company completed the acquisition of four complementary packaged gas
distributors with combined annual sales of approximately $33&nbsp;million. The
acquired businesses further expanded the Company&#146;s broad distribution network
and national market reach.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Strong cash flow fundamentals have been characteristics of the Company
since its inception. The cash flow generated by the Company, excluding the
cash generated from the expanded trade receivables securitization program that
was used to pay down the Company&#146;s revolving credit facilities, enabled it to
repay debt of $93.5&nbsp;million in fiscal 2003. Additionally, the Company was able
to reduce its off-balance sheet commitments by $2.5&nbsp;million in fiscal 2003.
Lower interest rates during fiscal 2003 also reduced the Company&#146;s debt service
requirements and aided in the ability to repay debt, as well as favorably
impacted net earnings during the year.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">21
</DIV>

primarily by the prior year acquisition of Air Products&#146; packaged gas business,
while same-store sales declined 2%. The intercompany eliminations represent
sales from All Other Operations to the Distribution segment.

</TABLE>

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Distribution segment sales increased $148&nbsp;million (10%) driven by
acquisitions, partially offset by a decline in same-store sales. The Company
estimates that net acquisition and divestiture activity contributed $181
million to sales in fiscal 2003. The net acquisition and divestiture activity
primarily reflects the impact of the February&nbsp;28, 2002 acquisition
of Air Products. The Air Products&#146; operations contributed $17.6&nbsp;million to the
Company&#146;s fiscal 2002 net sales. On a same-store basis, the Distribution
segment&#146;s sales decreased $33&nbsp;million (-2%) reflecting a decline in hardgoods
same-store sales of $42&nbsp;million (-5%), partially offset by gas and rent sales
growth of $9&nbsp;million (1%). The decline in hardgoods same-store sales resulted
from lower sales volumes of industrial tools and welding hardgoods, reflecting
the general weakness in the industrial and manufacturing sectors of the
economy. Although overall safety product sales were flat due to a decline in
branch-based sales, safety product sales through the telesales channel grew 4%.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Distribution gas and rent same-store sales growth was driven by sales
initiatives related to medical and bulk gases, as well as strategic accounts.
The growth achieved through these initiatives was partially offset by volume
declines in industrial gases reflecting the general weakness in the industrial
and manufacturing sectors of the economy. Fiscal 2003 medical gas and rent
revenues grew 8% to $135&nbsp;million versus the prior year reflecting volume gains.
Bulk gas and rent revenues increased to approximately $100&nbsp;million,
representing a 9% increase over the prior year with pricing remaining
relatively stable. On a same-store basis, sales to strategic account customers
(sales to large customers with multiple locations) grew 7% to $247&nbsp;million in
fiscal 2003 reflecting the Company&#146;s success in leveraging its broad
distribution network to service large customers. Although several major
strategic account customers purchased lower volumes of products, the addition
of nearly 80 new strategic account customers during fiscal 2003 generated sales
growth. Rental revenue was also favorably impacted by an 8% increase in
welding equipment rentals from the Company&#146;s expansion of its rental welder
fleet. The Company has followed a strategy of focusing on strategic sales
initiatives to drive sales growth and market penetration in the industries that
it serves. The strategic sales initiatives are designed to develop niches in
products and services that are expected to grow at a faster rate than the
overall economy and to position the Company for growth into the future.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;All Other Operations&#146; sales, net of intercompany sales eliminations,
increased $3.1&nbsp;million (2%), principally from net acquisition and divestiture
activity and same-store sales growth. Acquired sales of high-end specialty
gases from the Air Products acquisition more than offset divested sales from
two nitrous oxide plants sold in the third quarter of fiscal 2002. Same-store
sales increased $1.5&nbsp;million (1%) driven by specialty gas sales and higher
volumes of liquid carbon dioxide associated with the January&nbsp;2003 completion of
a new carbon dioxide plant in Hopewell, Virginia.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">22
</DIV>

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Distribution gross profits increased approximately $112&nbsp;million (15%)
primarily from the Air Products acquisition. The Distribution segment&#146;s gross
profit margin of 50.9% in fiscal 2003 increased 240 basis points from 48.5% in
fiscal 2002. The gross margin improvement resulted from a shift in sales mix
towards higher-margin gas and rent and reduced product discounting associated
with the Company&#146;s hardgoods discount management program. The shift in sales
mix was primarily attributable to the Air Products acquisition, which had a
sales mix of 76% gas and rent. On a same-store basis, lower volumes of
hardgoods also contributed to the shift in sales mix towards gas and rent.
Distribution segment sales consisted of 52.6% gas and rent compared to 47.3% in
the prior year.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;All Other Operations&#146; gross profits increased $7.8&nbsp;million (8%) driven by
lower raw material and production costs of the dry ice operations, which
reduced the cost per ton produced, and net acquisition and divestiture
activity. Gross profits attributable to specialty gas business acquired from
Air Products more than compensated for the fiscal 2002 divestiture of two
nitrous oxide plants. Same-store sales growth of specialty gases also
contributed to the increase in All Other Operations&#146; gross profits. All Other
Operations&#146; gross profit margin of 54.9% increased 130 basis points from 53.6%
in the prior year.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>Operating Expenses</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;SD&#038;A expenses increased $78.9&nbsp;million (13%) compared to fiscal 2002
principally from the addition of the Air Products&#146; operations. Although
salaries and benefits, fuel and acquisition integration costs increased in
fiscal 2003 compared to fiscal 2002, the increases were partially mitigated
by lower legal expenses and management&#146;s focus on expense control in other
areas. Legal expense in fiscal 2002 included a charge of $8.5&nbsp;million
associated with the settlement of litigation brought by a competitor. There
were no similar charges in fiscal 2003. As a percentage of net sales, SD&#038;A
expenses increased 120 basis points to 39.1% compared to 37.9% in the prior
year. The increase in operating expenses as a percentage of net sales reflects
the addition of the Air Products business, a majority of which is comprised of
the distribution of packaged gases. Packaged gas distribution typically
carries higher operating expenses than the hardgoods portion of the business
and correspondingly higher gross profit margins.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Depreciation expense of $73.5&nbsp;million in fiscal 2003 increased $8.7
million (13%) compared to $64.8&nbsp;million in fiscal 2002. The increase was
primarily due to depreciation on the addition of the fixed assets from the Air
Products acquisition.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Amortization expense of $6.4&nbsp;million in fiscal 2003 decreased $1.8&nbsp;million
compared to $8.2&nbsp;million in fiscal 2002. The decrease in amortization expense
was primarily attributable to the expiration of certain non-compete agreements.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">23
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->

<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>Special Charge</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;A special charge of $2.7&nbsp;million incurred by the Distribution segment in
fiscal 2003 consisted of a restructuring charge related to the integration of
the business acquired from Air Products and costs related to the consolidation
of certain hardgoods procurement functions. The special charge included
facility exit costs associated with the closure of certain facilities and
employee severance. The facilities exited and the affected employees were part
of the Company&#146;s existing operations prior to the acquisition of the Air
Products business.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>Operating Income</I>
