<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(2)&nbsp;ACCOUNTING AND DISCLOSURE CHANGES &#150; (Continued)



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>SFAS 141</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Effective July&nbsp;1, 2001, the Company adopted SFAS No.&nbsp;141, <I>Business
Combinations</I>. SFAS 141 requires that the purchase method of accounting be used
for all business combinations initiated after June&nbsp;30, 2001. The adoption of
SFAS 141 did not have an impact on the historical results of operations,
financial position or liquidity of the Company.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>SFAS 142</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In July&nbsp;2001, the FASB issued SFAS No.&nbsp;142, <I>Goodwill and Other Intangible
Assets</I>. As permitted under the Statement, the Company adopted SFAS 142
effective April&nbsp;1, 2001. SFAS 142 requires goodwill and intangible assets with
indefinite useful lives to no longer be amortized, but instead be tested for
impairment at least annually. Upon adoption of SFAS 142, the Company performed
an evaluation of goodwill, which indicated that goodwill recorded in the
Distribution segment associated with its industrial tool reporting unit was
impaired as of April&nbsp;1, 2001. Accordingly, the Company recognized a $59
million non-cash charge, recorded as of April&nbsp;1, 2001, as the cumulative effect
of a change in accounting principle for the write-down of goodwill to its fair
value. The impaired goodwill was not deductible for taxes, and as a result, no
tax benefit was recorded in relation to the charge.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;With the adoption of SFAS 142, the Company reassessed the useful lives and
residual values of all acquired intangible assets to make any necessary
amortization period adjustments. Based on that assessment, no adjustments were
made to the amortization period or residual values of other intangible assets.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;SFAS 142 also requires goodwill to be tested annually and between annual
tests if events occur or circumstances change that would more likely than not
reduce the fair value of a reporting unit below its carrying amount. The
Company has elected to perform its annual tests for indications of goodwill
impairment as of October&nbsp;31 of each year. As of October&nbsp;31, 2003 and 2002, the
Company&#146;s annual assessment of each of its reporting units indicated that
goodwill was not impaired.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>SFAS 143</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In July&nbsp;2001, the FASB issued SFAS No.&nbsp;143, <I>Accounting for Asset
Retirement Obligations</I>. SFAS 143 requires the recognition of a liability for
an asset retirement obligation in the period in which it is incurred. A
retirement obligation is defined as one in which a legal obligation exists in
the future resulting from existing laws, statutes or contracts. The Company
adopted SFAS 143 on April&nbsp;1, 2003, as required. The adoption of SFAS 143 did
not have a material impact on its results of operations, financial position or
liquidity.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>SFAS 144</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;On April&nbsp;1, 2002, the Company adopted SFAS No.&nbsp;144, <I>Accounting for the
Impairment or Disposal of Long-Lived Assets</I>, as required. SFAS 144 addresses
financial accounting and reporting for the impairment or disposal of long-lived
assets, including discontinued operations. The adoption of SFAS 144 did not
have a material impact on the Company&#146;s consolidated financial position,
results of operations or liquidity.

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>SFAS 146</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;On January&nbsp;1, 2003, the Company adopted SFAS No.&nbsp;146, <I>Accounting for Costs
Associated with Exit or Disposal Activities</I>, as required, which is effective
for exit or disposal activities initiated after December&nbsp;31, 2002. SFAS 146
nullifies Emerging Issues Task Force (&#147;EITF&#148;) Issue No.&nbsp;94-3, <I>Liability
Recognition for Certain Employee Termination Benefits and Other Costs to Exit
an Activity (including Certain Costs Incurred in a Restructuring). </I>SFAS 146
addresses financial accounting and reporting for costs associated with exit or
disposal activities, including restructuring activities. The Statement
requires that a liability for a cost associated with an exit or disposal
activity be recognized when the liability is incurred as opposed to at the time
the Company commits to an exit plan as previously permitted under EITF 94-3.
The adoption of SFAS 146 did not have a material impact on the Company&#146;s
consolidated financial position, results of operations or liquidity.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>SFAS 148</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In December&nbsp;2002, the FASB issued SFAS No.&nbsp;148, <I>Accounting for Stock-Based
Compensation &#150; Transition and Disclosure</I>. Based on the Statement&#146;s effective
date of fiscal years ending after December&nbsp;31, 2002, the Company adopted the
provisions of SFAS 148 in fiscal 2003. SFAS 148 amends SFAS 123, <I>Accounting
for Stock-Based Compensation</I>, to provide alternative methods of transition for
an entity that voluntarily changes to the fair value basis of accounting for
stock-based employee compensation. The Statement also amends the disclosure
provisions of SFAS 123 to require prominent disclosure about the effects on
reported net income of an entity&#146;s accounting policy decisions with respect to
stock-based employee compensation. The Company has elected to continue to
account for its stock-based compensation plans using the intrinsic value method
of accounting as permitted by Accounting Principles Board Opinion No.&nbsp;25,
<I>Accounting for Stock Issued to Employees</I>, but has adopted the disclosure
requirements of SFAS 148 (see Note 15).


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>SFAS 149</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In April&nbsp;2003, the FASB issued SFAS No.&nbsp;149, <I>Amendment of Statement 133 on
Derivative Instruments and Hedging Activities. </I>SFAS 149 amends and clarifies
financial accounting and reporting for derivative instruments, including
certain derivative instruments embedded in other contracts and for hedging
activities under SFAS 133, <I>Accounting for Derivative Instruments and Hedging
Activities</I>. SFAS 149 requires, among other things, that contracts with
comparable characteristics be accounted for similarly and clarifies the
circumstances under which a contract with an initial net investment meets the
characteristic of a derivative and when a derivative contains a financing
component that warrants special reporting in the statement of cash flows. The
Company adopted SFAS 149 on July&nbsp;1, 2003, as required. The adoption of SFAS
149 did not have a material impact on its results of operations, financial
position or liquidity.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>SFAS 150</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In May&nbsp;2003, the FASB issued SFAS No.&nbsp;150, <I>Accounting for Certain
Financial Instruments with Characteristics of both Liabilities and Equity</I>.
SFAS 150 establishes standards for how an issuer classifies and measures
certain financial instruments with characteristics of both liabilities and
equity in the statement of financial position. SFAS 150 requires that an
issuer classify a financial instrument that is within its scope as a liability
(or an asset in some circumstances). Many of those financial instruments were
previously classified as equity. The Company adopted SFAS 150 on July&nbsp;1, 2003,
as required. The adoption of SFAS 150 did not have a material impact on its
results of operations, financial position or liquidity.

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>AIRGAS, INC. AND SUBSIDIARIES<BR>
NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS</B>



<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt">(2)&nbsp;ACCOUNTING AND DISCLOSURE CHANGES &#150; (Continued)


<P align="left" style="margin-left: 0%; text-indent: 0%; margin-right: 0%; font-size: 10pt"><I>FASB Financial Interpretation No.&nbsp;45</I>


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In November&nbsp;2002, the FASB issued Financial Interpretation No.&nbsp;45,
<I>Guarantor&#146;s Accounting and Disclosure Requirements for Guarantees, Including
Indirect Guarantees of Indebtedness of Others</I>, (&#147;FIN 45&#148;). FIN 45 elaborates
on the disclosures to be made by a guarantor in its interim and annual
financial statements about its obligations under certain guarantees that it has
issued. It also clarifies that a guarantor is required to recognize, at the
inception of a guarantee, a liability for the fair value of the obligation
undertaken in issuing the guarantee. The initial recognition and measurement
provisions of FIN 45 are applicable on a prospective basis to guarantees issued
or modified after December&nbsp;31, 2002.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Based on a review of the Company&#146;s material contracts and finance
agreements, the only additional disclosure required by the interpretation
relates to the Company&#146;s residual value guarantee associated with certain
leased vehicles (see Note 21).


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>FASB Financial Interpretation No.&nbsp;46</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In January&nbsp;2003, the FASB issued Financial Interpretation No.&nbsp;46,
<I>Consolidation of Variable Interest Entities, </I>(&#147;FIN 46&#148;). The interpretation was
initially effective for the first interim period beginning after June&nbsp;15, 2003.
However, as a result of implementation issues, in December&nbsp;2003, the FASB
issued FASB Interpretation No.&nbsp;46 (revised December&nbsp;2003), <I>Consolidation of
Variable Interest Entities, </I>(&#147;FIN 46R&#148;), which was effective as of the end of
the first reporting period ending after March&nbsp;15, 2004, with early adoption
permitted.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;FIN 46 and FIN 46R addresses consolidation by a business enterprise of
variable interest entities. Variable interest entities are defined as
corporations, partnerships, trusts, or any other legal structure used for
business purposes, and by design, the holders of equity instruments in those
entities lack one of the characteristics of a financial controlling interest.
FIN 46 and FIN 46R changes previous accounting practice by introducing the
concept of a &#147;Primary Beneficiary&#148; and requires variable interest entities to
be consolidated by the party deemed to be the Primary Beneficiary (i.e., the
party that is subject to a majority of the risk of loss from the variable
interest entity&#146;s activities or entitled to receive a majority of the entity&#146;s
residual returns or both). Under previous accounting practice, entities
generally were not consolidated unless the entity was controlled through voting
interests.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Since October&nbsp;1999, the Company has leased certain real estate and
equipment from a grantor trust (the &#147;Trust&#148;) under a sale-leaseback
arrangement. The Trust incurred debt to purchase the properties and equipment
from the Company. The Company, in turn, paid rent to the Trust. The rental
payments to the Trust were based on LIBOR plus an applicable margin and matched
the debt service requirements for the debt held by the Trust. The Trust was
not consolidated for financial reporting purposes and the non-cancelable lease
obligation of the real estate and equipment leases totaled approximately $41
million at March&nbsp;31, 2003.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Effective July&nbsp;1, 2003, the Company elected to early adopt FIN 46 with
respect to the Trust. The Company determined the Trust to be a variable
interest entity as defined by FIN 46. In addition, the Company was
determined to be the primary beneficiary of the sale-leaseback arrangement.
FIN 46 required the Company to consolidate the Trust for financial reporting
purposes. Upon consolidation, the Company recorded on its balance sheet
approximately $29&nbsp;million of real estate and equipment and debt of $42
million, while eliminating a long-term deferred gain of $13&nbsp;million. The
consolidation of the Trust resulted in the Company recognizing $1&nbsp;million in
interest expense and $1&nbsp;million in depreciation expense in the nine months
ended March&nbsp;31, 2004, which had previously been recognized as rent expense to
the Trust. The debt of the grantor trust matures in October&nbsp;2004 and is
classified as long-term based upon the Company&#146;s ability and intention to
refinance it with borrowings under its long-term credit facilities.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">F-14
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>AIRGAS, INC. AND SUBSIDIARIES<BR>
NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS</B>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(2)&nbsp;ACCOUNTING AND DISCLOSURE CHANGES &#150; (Continued)



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Since June&nbsp;1996, the Company has participated in a joint venture with
National Welders Supply Company, Inc. (&#147;National Welders&#148;), a producer and
distributor of industrial gases based in Charlotte, North Carolina. The
Company determined that National Welders meets the definition of a &#147;Variable
Interest Entity&#148; under FIN 46R and that the Company is the Primary Beneficiary
of the joint venture. Therefore, effective December&nbsp;31, 2003, the Company
elected to adopt FIN 46R, as it applies to the joint venture, and consolidated
National Welders. As permitted by FIN 46R, the Company applied the
interpretation prospectively from the date of adoption. Therefore, at December
31, 2003, the consolidation of National Welders only affected the balance
sheet. There was no cumulative effect adjustment or impact
on cash flows as a result of the consolidation. The consolidation had the
effect of eliminating the Company&#146;s $62&nbsp;million
investment in National Welders and recording the joint venture&#146;s assets,
liabilities and a corresponding minority interest liability. The assets and
liabilities of National Welders included goodwill of $56&nbsp;million and debt of
$62&nbsp;million, which is non-recourse to the Company.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Beginning January&nbsp;1, 2004, National Welders&#146; operating results were no
longer reflected as &#147;Equity in Earnings of Unconsolidated Affiliates.&#148; Rather,
the operating results were reflected broadly across the income statement with
minority interest expense representing the quarterly dividend on the joint
venture&#146;s redeemable preferred stock, net of interest earned on a note
receivable from the preferred stockholders. The joint venture is structured
such that the Company earns the residual net income available to the common
stockholder, which is net of the minority interest expense. Since the
allocation of the joint venture&#146;s net earnings was unaffected by the adoption
of FIN 46R, the consolidation of National Welders did not impact the Company&#146;s
net earnings. See Note 16 for additional disclosures regarding National
Welders.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(3)&nbsp;ACQUISITIONS AND DIVESTITURES



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(a) <I>Acquisitions</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Acquisitions have been recorded using the purchase method of accounting
and, accordingly, results of their operations have been included in the
Company&#146;s consolidated financial statements since the effective date of each
respective acquisition. The purchase price for business acquisitions was
allocated to the assets acquired and liabilities assumed based on their
estimated fair values at the date of acquisition. Such allocations were based
on preliminary estimates of fair value at the date of acquisition, which may be
revised at a later date.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">Fiscal 2004



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;During fiscal 2004, the Company purchased five businesses associated with
the distribution of packaged gases and related hardgoods products and dry ice.
The largest of these businesses and their effective dates of acquisition
included Delta Safety Supply, Inc. (May&nbsp;1, 2003) and Interstate Welding Sales
Corporation (March&nbsp;31, 2004). The aggregate purchase price of the fiscal 2004
acquisitions was $41&nbsp;million, including assumed liabilities. Four of the
businesses had aggregate annual revenues of approximately $57&nbsp;million and were
included in the Distribution segment. One acquired dry ice distribution
business generated annual revenues of approximately $2&nbsp;million and was included
in All Other Operations. The Company negotiated the respective purchase prices
of the businesses based on the expected cash flows to be derived from their
operations after integration into the Company&#146;s existing distribution network.
The Company acquired the businesses to expand its geographic coverage and
strengthen its national network of branch store locations. Total assets
contributed by fiscal 2004 acquisitions were approximately $41&nbsp;million. The
excess of the purchase price of acquired businesses over the net assets
acquired (&#147;goodwill&#148;) related to fiscal 2004 acquisitions amounted to
approximately $10&nbsp;million. Intangible assets, other than goodwill, totaled
approximately $5&nbsp;million and primarily related to customer lists and
non-compete agreements obtained from the sellers of the businesses.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">F-15
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>AIRGAS, INC. AND SUBSIDIARIES<BR>
NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS</B>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(3)&nbsp;ACQUISITIONS AND DIVESTITURES &#150; (Continued)



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;For the fiscal 2004 acquisitions, additional work related to the
performance of asset appraisals needs to be completed to finalize the
allocation of the purchase price to net assets, identified intangibles and
goodwill acquired. The entire purchase price ultimately assigned to goodwill
will be deductible for income taxes. The Company does not expect the final
allocation of the purchase price to differ materially from the amounts included
in the accompanying consolidated financial statements.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">Fiscal 2003



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;During fiscal 2003, the Company purchased four businesses that distribute
packaged gases and related equipment and hardgoods products with combined
annual revenues of $33&nbsp;million. The largest of these businesses and their
effective dates of acquisition included Welding Metals, Inc. (November&nbsp;27,
2002) and Union Industrial Gas and Supply, Inc. (March&nbsp;31, 2003). The
aggregate purchase price of all four acquisitions was $23.8&nbsp;million, including
assumed liabilities. The acquired businesses have been included in the
Distribution segment from their effective dates of acquisition. Goodwill
related to fiscal 2003 acquisitions totaled approximately $6.4&nbsp;million and was
deductible for income taxes.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">Fiscal 2002



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;On February&nbsp;28, 2002, the Company purchased the majority of Air Products
and Chemicals, Inc.&#146;s (&#147;Air Products&#148;) U.S. packaged gas business, excluding
its electronic gases and magnetic resonance imaging-related helium operations,
for cash of approximately $247&nbsp;million, including transaction costs. The
transaction was financed entirely with senior bank debt. The acquisition
included 88 facilities in 30 states associated with the filling and
distribution of cylinders, liquid dewars, tube trailers, and other containers
of industrial gases and non-electronic specialty gases, and the selling of
welding hardgoods, including customer service centers, warehouses, and other
related assets. The Company acquired the business based on its strategic fit
with the Company&#146;s existing packaged gas distribution business and to
strengthen its national network by giving it a presence in important
geographies. Additionally, the acquisition strengthened the core competencies
of the Company&#146;s specialty gas operations, enabling it to enhance its
competitiveness. These operations historically generated annual revenues of
approximately $220&nbsp;million and had 1,100 employees. Air Products also sold its
packaged gas operations in the Carolinas and southern Virginia to National
Welders. Goodwill related to the Air Products acquisition amounted to
approximately $48&nbsp;million, of which $25.9&nbsp;million was recognized in fiscal
2003. The fiscal 2003 increase in goodwill was the result of adjustments to
the March&nbsp;31, 2002 preliminary purchase price allocation. The revised purchase
price allocation was based on estimates of fair values determined primarily
through third party appraisals. Third party appraisals and cylinder and other
fixed asset inventories related to the Air Products acquisition were finalized
during fiscal 2003.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Additionally, in September&nbsp;2001, the Company acquired six retail
distributor locations from Air Liquide America Corporation (&#147;Air Liquide&#148;).
The purchase price of $11&nbsp;million resulted in goodwill of approximately $7
million. The goodwill associated with the Air Liquide transaction was entirely
deductible for income taxes. These operations added annual revenues of
approximately $10&nbsp;million.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">F-16
</DIV>

prepared for comparative purposes only and do not purport to be indicative of
what would have occurred had the acquisitions been made as of April&nbsp;1, 2001 or
of results that may occur in the future.

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(b) <I>Divestitures</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In fiscal 2002, the carrying values of the net assets of Kendeco, Inc.
(&#147;Kendeco&#148;), an industrial tool business in the Distribution operating segment,
were reduced by $3.6&nbsp;million to their net realizable value based on the
estimated selling price less cost to sell. The loss, reflected in &#147;Other
income (expense), net,&#148; included the write-off of $3.1&nbsp;million of non-tax
deductible goodwill. The Company completed the sale of Kendeco in May&nbsp;2002
(fiscal 2003) and recognized a loss on the sale of $1.7&nbsp;million. Kendeco
contributed sales of $17.7&nbsp;million and operating income of $158 thousand in
fiscal 2002. Kendeco&#146;s fiscal 2003 operating results were insignificant.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In fiscal 2000, the Company divested its investment in a Polish gas
distributor. As part of the sale agreement, the Company made representations
and warranties about the business being sold. Subsequently, the acquirer
presented the Company with an indemnity claim as a breach of the
representations and warranties provided for under the sale agreement. In
fiscal 2002, the Company recorded a liability and related expense of $1.9
million associated with the indemnity claim. In fiscal 2003, the Company
favorably resolved the indemnity claim and reversed $1.5&nbsp;million of expense,
net of legal fees paid, and the related liability. These items are reflected
in &#147;Other income (expense), net&#148; in the respective fiscal years.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In October&nbsp;2001, the Company sold two of its nitrous oxide production
facilities to Air Liquide. The Company retained its remaining four nitrous
oxide production facilities to meet its requirements as a major producer and
distributor of nitrous oxide. After-tax proceeds from the sale of
approximately $10&nbsp;million were used to reduce borrowings under the Company&#146;s
revolving credit facilities. The Company recognized a gain on the transaction
of $7.4&nbsp;million in &#147;Other income (expense), net&#148; in fiscal 2002. The nitrous
oxide facilities were reflected in the operating results of the All Other
Operations segment. The divested operations generated sales of $3.5&nbsp;million
and operating income of $1.2&nbsp;million in fiscal 2002.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">F-17
</DIV>

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(4)&nbsp;SPECIAL CHARGES (RECOVERIES), NET



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;During the fourth quarter of fiscal 2004, the Company recorded a special
charge recovery of $776 thousand (the &#147;2004 Special Recovery&#148;) consisting of
lower estimates of the ultimate cost of prior years&#146; restructuring charges.
The 2004 Special Recovery pertained to a change in estimate related to facility
exit costs.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;During fiscal 2003, the Company recorded a special charge of $2.7&nbsp;million
(the &#147;2003 Special Charge&#148;) consisting of a restructuring charge related to the
integration of the business acquired from Air Products during the fourth
quarter of fiscal 2002 and costs related to the consolidation of certain
hardgoods procurement functions. The special charge included facility exit
costs associated with the closure of certain facilities and severance. The
facilities exited and the affected employees were part of the Company&#146;s
existing operations prior to the acquisition of the Air Products business.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;During fiscal 2002, the Company announced the transition of certain
administrative functions to a financial shared services center. The Company
recorded a special charge of approximately $1&nbsp;million (the &#147;2002 Special
Charge&#148;) for severance costs and costs related to exiting one facility
associated with the fiscal 2003-2004 transition to the shared services center.

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(5)&nbsp;EARNINGS (LOSS)&nbsp;PER SHARE



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Basic earnings (loss)&nbsp;per share is calculated by dividing net earnings
(loss)&nbsp;by the weighted average number of shares of the Company&#146;s common stock
outstanding during the period. Outstanding shares consist of issued shares
less treasury stock and common stock held by the Employee Benefits Trust.
Diluted earnings (loss)&nbsp;per share is calculated by dividing net earnings (loss)
by the weighted average common shares outstanding adjusted for the dilutive
effect of common stock equivalents related to stock options and warrants.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The table below reconciles basic weighted average common shares
outstanding to diluted weighted average common shares outstanding for the three
years ended March&nbsp;31, 2004, 2003 and 2002:

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Pursuant to a joint venture agreement between the Company and the holders
of the preferred stock of National Welders, between June&nbsp;30, 2006 and June&nbsp;30,
2009, the preferred shareholders have the option to exchange their 3.2&nbsp;million
preferred shares of National Welders either for cash at a price of $17.78 per
share or to tender them to the joint venture in exchange for approximately 2.3
million shares of Airgas common stock (see Note 16). If Airgas common stock
has a market value of $24.45 per share, the stock and cash redemption options
are equivalent. No contingently issuable shares are included in the diluted
weighted average common shares calculation (the &#147;diluted computation&#148;), as the
conversion of the National Welders preferred stock in exchange for the
Company&#146;s common stock is anti-dilutive.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Outstanding stock options and warrants, with an exercise price above
market, are excluded from the Company&#146;s diluted computation as their effect
would be anti-dilutive. There were approximately 545 thousand, 2&nbsp;million and
2.6&nbsp;million outstanding stock options and warrants with an exercise price above
the average market price at March&nbsp;31, 2004, 2003, and 2002, respectively. If
the average market value of the Company&#146;s common stock increases above the
respective exercise prices of the options and warrants, they will be included
in the diluted computation as common stock equivalents.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">F-19

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Net inventories determined by the LIFO inventory method totaled $26.8
million and $15.7&nbsp;million at March&nbsp;31, 2004 and 2003, respectively. If the
FIFO inventory method had been used for these inventories, they would have been
$3&nbsp;million and $1.4&nbsp;million higher at March&nbsp;31, 2004 and 2003, respectively.
Substantially all of the inventories are finished goods. The increase in net
inventories determined by the LIFO inventory method resulted from the
consolidation of National Welders (see Notes 2 and 16).

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(8)&nbsp;GOODWILL AND OTHER INTANGIBLE ASSETS

<DIV align="center">

agreements, principally five years. There are no expected residual values
related to these intangible assets. Estimated future amortization expense by
fiscal year is as follows: 2005 &#151; $5.8&nbsp;million; 2006 &#151; $4.4&nbsp;million; 2007 -
$3.2&nbsp;million; 2008 &#151; $2.4&nbsp;million; 2009 &#151; $1.1&nbsp;million and $2.8&nbsp;million
thereafter.

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>Revolving Credit Facilities</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company has unsecured revolving credit facilities with a syndicate of
lenders totaling $367.5&nbsp;million and $50&nbsp;million Canadian (US $38.2&nbsp;million).
The revolving credit facilities will mature on July&nbsp;30, 2006. At March&nbsp;31,
2004, the Company had borrowings under the revolving facilities of
approximately $8&nbsp;million and $25&nbsp;million Canadian (U.S. $19&nbsp;million). The
Company also had commitments under letters of credit supported by the revolving
credit facilities of approximately $33&nbsp;million at March&nbsp;31, 2004. Based on
restrictions related to certain leverage ratios, the Company had additional
borrowing capacity under the revolving facilities of approximately $225&nbsp;million
at March&nbsp;31, 2004. The variable interest rates of the U.S. and Canadian
revolving credit facilities are based on the London Interbank Offered Rate
(&#147;LIBOR&#148;) and Canadian Bankers&#146; Acceptance Rates, respectively. At March&nbsp;31,
2004, the effective interest rates on borrowings under the revolving facilities
were 3.13% on U.S. borrowings and 4.44% on Canadian borrowings.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Borrowings under the revolving credit facilities are guaranteed by certain
of the Company&#146;s domestic subsidiaries and Canadian borrowings are guaranteed
by Canadian subsidiaries. The Company pledged 100% of the stock of its
domestic guarantor subsidiaries and 65% of the stock of its foreign guarantor
subsidiaries for the benefit of the syndicate of lenders. If the Company&#146;s
credit rating is reduced, the Company will be required to grant a security
interest in substantially all of the tangible and intangible assets of the
Company for the benefit of the syndicate of lenders. The revolving credit
facilities also contain covenants, which include the maintenance of certain
leverage ratios, a fixed charge ratio, and potential restrictions on certain
additional borrowing, the amount of dividends declared and paid, and the
repurchase of common stock.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In May&nbsp;2003, the Company obtained an amendment to its credit agreement
that allowed for the issuance of up to an additional $200&nbsp;million of senior
public debt and for the expansion of its senior credit facilities by up to $150
million (see <I>Senior Subordinated Notes </I>below). Subject to existing financial
covenants, the amendment also provided the Company with additional flexibility
to pay dividends, repurchase Company common stock and to invest in
acquisitions.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In anticipation of the acquisition of assets from The BOC Group, Inc.
(&#147;BOC&#148;) (see Note 26), the Company obtained an amendment to its credit
agreement in February&nbsp;2004 that, among other things, permits the Company to
invest up to $275&nbsp;million in acquisitions during fiscal 2005.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">F-22
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>AIRGAS, INC. AND SUBSIDIARIES<BR>
NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS</B>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(10)&nbsp;INDEBTEDNESS &#150; (Continued)



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>Term Loan</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;At March&nbsp;31, 2004, the Company had an outstanding term loan with a
principal balance of $70&nbsp;million bearing an effective interest rate of 3.11%.
The term loan is due in quarterly installments with a final payment due July
30, 2006. The term loan is unsecured and bears a variable interest rate based
on LIBOR plus a spread related to the Company&#146;s credit rating. Principal
payments on the term loan are classified as &#147;Long-term Debt&#148; in the Company&#146;s
Consolidated Balance Sheets based on the Company&#146;s ability and intention to
refinance the payments with borrowings under its long-term revolving credit
facilities.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>Medium-Term Notes</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;At March&nbsp;31, 2004, the Company had $100&nbsp;million of unsecured medium-term
notes outstanding due September&nbsp;2006 and bearing interest at a fixed rate of
7.75%. The medium-term notes are guaranteed by each of the domestic guarantors
under the revolving credit facilities.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>Acquisition and Other Notes</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Acquisition and other notes principally consist of notes issued to sellers
of businesses acquired and are repayable in periodic installments. At March
31, 2004, acquisition and other notes totaled $8.5&nbsp;million with interest rates
ranging from 4% to 9%.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>Senior Subordinated Notes</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In March&nbsp;2004, the Company issued $150&nbsp;million of senior subordinated
notes (the &#147;2004 Notes&#148;) with a maturity date of July&nbsp;15, 2014. The 2004 Notes
bear interest at a fixed annual rate of 6.25%, payable semi-annually on January
15 and July&nbsp;15 of each year. Proceeds from the issuance of the 2004 Notes were
used to redeem $75&nbsp;million of medium-term notes due March&nbsp;2004 and to pay down
borrowings on the Company&#146;s U.S. revolving credit facility. The 2004 Notes
were sold in accordance with the provisions of Rule&nbsp;144A under the Securities
Act of 1933 (the &#147;Securities Act&#148;). During fiscal 2005, the Company will
exchange the 2004 Notes for substantially similar notes registered with the
Securities and Exchange Commission in accordance with the Securities Act.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In addition to the 2004 Notes, at March&nbsp;31, 2004, the Company had $225
million of senior subordinated notes (the &#147;2001 Notes&#148;) outstanding with a
maturity date of October&nbsp;1, 2011. The 2001 Notes bear interest at a fixed
annual rate of 9.125%, payable semi-annually on April 1 and October 1 of each
year.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The 2004 and 2001 Notes contain covenants that could restrict the payment
of dividends, the repurchase of common stock, the issuance of preferred stock,
and the incurrence of additional indebtedness and liens. The 2004 and 2001
Notes are guaranteed on a subordinated basis by each of the domestic guarantors
under the revolving credit facilities.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">F-23
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>AIRGAS, INC. AND SUBSIDIARIES<BR>
NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS</B>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(10)&nbsp;INDEBTEDNESS &#150; (Continued)



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>Debt of Grantor Trust</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Since October&nbsp;1999, the Company has leased certain real estate and
equipment from a grantor trust (the &#147;Trust&#148;) under a sale-leaseback
arrangement. The Trust incurred debt to purchase the properties and equipment
from the Company. The Company, in turn, paid rent to the Trust. The rental
payments to the Trust were based on LIBOR plus an applicable margin and matched
the debt service requirements for the debt held by the Trust. The Trust was
not consolidated for financial reporting purposes and the non-cancelable lease
obligation of the real estate and equipment leases totaled approximately $41
million at March&nbsp;31, 2003.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Effective July&nbsp;1, 2003, the Company elected to early adopt FIN 46 with
respect to the Trust (see Note 2). The Company determined the Trust to be a
variable interest entity as defined by FIN 46. In addition, the Company was
determined to be the primary beneficiary of the sale-leaseback arrangement.
FIN 46 required the Company to consolidate the Trust for financial reporting
purposes. Upon consolidation, the Company recorded on its balance sheet
approximately $29&nbsp;million of real estate and equipment and debt of $42
million, while eliminating a long-term deferred gain of $13&nbsp;million. The
consolidation of the Trust resulted in the Company recognizing $1&nbsp;million in
interest expense and $1&nbsp;million in depreciation expense in the nine months
ended March&nbsp;31, 2004, which had previously been recognized as rent expense to
the Trust. The debt of the grantor trust matures in October&nbsp;2004 and is
classified as long-term based upon the Company&#146;s ability and intention to
refinance it with borrowings under its long-term credit facilities.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>Debt of the National Welders Joint Venture</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Effective December&nbsp;31, 2003, the Company adopted FIN 46R and consolidated
its National Welders joint venture. In accordance with the requirements of FIN
46R, the Company&#146;s Consolidated Balance Sheet at March&nbsp;31, 2004 includes the
financial obligations of the National Welders joint venture. National Welders&#146;
financial debt instruments are non-recourse to the Company, meaning that the
creditors of National Welders do not have a claim on the assets of Airgas, Inc.
in settlement of the joint venture&#146;s debt obligations. The debt of National
Welders consists of:

Welders. The credit agreement provides for a Term Loan A of $26.2&nbsp;million, a
Term Loan B of $21&nbsp;million, a Term Loan C of $8.8&nbsp;million, and a revolving
credit line of $44&nbsp;million. Term Loan A is repayable in monthly amounts of
$254 thousand through maturity in June&nbsp;2007. The Term Loan B matures in July
2006. Amounts outstanding at March&nbsp;31, 2004 under Term Loans A and B were $21
million and $21&nbsp;million, respectively. There were no amounts borrowed or
outstanding under Term Loan C at March&nbsp;31, 2004. The revolving credit line
matures in June&nbsp;2006. Interest rates on the Credit Agreement are variable and
range from LIBOR plus 150 to 225 basis points based on
National Welders&#146; leverage ratio. The Credit Agreement contains certain
covenants which, among other things, limit the

on the preferred stock. Additionally, the common stock dividends must be
declared by a vote of the joint venture&#146;s board of directors.

certain leverage ratios, National Welders had additional borrowing capacity
under its Credit Agreement of $47.3&nbsp;million at March&nbsp;31, 2004. Acquisition and
other notes principally consisted of a note payable to a bank that is secured
by a production facility and bears a fixed interest rate of 7%.

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(11)&nbsp;FAIR VALUE OF FINANCIAL INSTRUMENTS



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Summarized below are the carrying and fair values of the Company&#146;s
financial instruments at March&nbsp;31, 2004 and 2003.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The fair value of the Company&#146;s publicly traded financial instruments is
based on market pricing. The fair value of other non-publicly traded financial
instruments is based on estimates using standard pricing models that take into
account the present value of future cash flows as of the balance sheet date.
The computation of fair values of these instruments is generally performed by
the Company. The carrying amounts reported in the balance sheet for trade
receivables and payables, accrued liabilities, accrued income taxes, and
short-term borrowings approximate fair value due to the short-term nature of
these instruments. Accordingly, these items have been excluded from the table
below:

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(12)&nbsp;DERIVATIVE INSTRUMENTS AND HEDGING ACTIVITIES



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company manages its exposure to changes in market interest rates. The
Company&#146;s involvement with derivative instruments is limited to highly
effective fixed and floating interest rate swap agreements used to manage
well-defined interest rate risk exposures. Interest rate swap agreements are
not entered into for trading purposes. As described in Note 2, the Company
adopted SFAS 133 as of April&nbsp;1, 2001.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;At March&nbsp;31, 2004, the Company had four fixed interest rate swap
agreements with a notional amount of $90&nbsp;million that effectively convert a
corresponding amount of variable interest rate
debt of a consolidated grantor trust (see Note 10) and trade receivables securitization
agreement (see Note 13). The swap agreements require the Company to make fixed
interest payments based on an average effective rate of 4.55% and receive
variable interest payments from its counterparties based on three-month LIBOR
(average rate of 1.12% at March&nbsp;31, 2004). The remaining terms of these swap
agreements range from between four and nineteen months. In fiscal 2004 and
2003, the Company recorded a net change in the fair value of the fixed interest
rate swap agreements of $2&nbsp;million and $565 thousand as accumulated other
comprehensive income, respectively. The net additional interest payments made
under these swap agreements during fiscal 2004 were recognized in interest
expense. Over the next 12&nbsp;months, the Company expects to reclassify $1.9
million of the deferred loss from accumulated other comprehensive loss to
interest expense as the related interest payments that are being hedged are
recognized.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">F-26
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>AIRGAS, INC. AND SUBSIDIARIES<BR>
NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS</B>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(12)&nbsp;DERIVATIVE INSTRUMENTS AND HEDGING ACTIVITIES &#150; (Continued)



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;At March&nbsp;31, 2004, the Company also had four variable interest rate swap
agreements with a notional amount of $125&nbsp;million that effectively converts a
corresponding amount of fixed rate medium-term and senior subordinated notes to
variable rate debt. The swap agreements require the Company to make variable
interest payments based primarily on six-month LIBOR (average rate of 3.16% at
March&nbsp;31, 2004) and receive fixed interest payments from its counterparties
based on an average effective rate of 8.36% at March&nbsp;31, 2004. The remaining
terms of these swap agreements range from between two and seven years. The
fair value of these variable interest rate swap agreements and the increased
carrying value of the hedged portions of the medium-term and senior
subordinated notes at March&nbsp;31, 2004 was $13.8&nbsp;million. The changes in the
fair value of the swap agreements are offset by changes in the fair value of
the hedged portions of the medium-term and senior subordinated notes.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The effect of these interest rate swap agreements was to adjust the
Company&#146;s ratio of fixed to variable interest rates to 57% fixed and 43%
variable and to reduce interest expense by approximately $4.7&nbsp;million and $3.4
million in fiscal 2004 and 2003, respectively, and increase interest expense by
$2.9&nbsp;million in fiscal 2002.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>National Welders&#146; Hedging Activities</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company&#146;s National Welders joint venture affiliate participates in one
interest rate swap with a notional principal amount of $21&nbsp;million that
effectively converts a corresponding amount of variable interest rate debt
(Term Loan B &#151; see Note 10) to a fixed rate debt instrument. The interest rate
swap requires National Welders to make interest payments based on a fixed rate
of 6.72% and receivable variable interest payments from its counterparty based
on a floating LIBOR rate of 1.09% at March&nbsp;31, 2004. The interest rate swap
agreement was not entered into for trading purposes. The consolidation of
National Welders resulted in the addition of a cumulative unrealized loss on
the National Welders interest rate swap of $2.5&nbsp;million and associated tax
benefit of $1&nbsp;million in &#147;Accumulated Other Comprehensive Loss.&#148;


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>Aggregate Maturities of Interest Rate Swap Agreements</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Including National Welders, the aggregate maturities of the Company&#146;s

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>AIRGAS, INC. AND SUBSIDIARIES<BR>
NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS</B>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(13)&nbsp;TRADE RECEIVABLES SECURITIZATION



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company participates in a securitization agreement with two commercial
banks to sell up to $175&nbsp;million of qualifying trade receivables. The
agreement will expire in December&nbsp;2005, but may be renewed subject to renewal
provisions contained in the agreement. During fiscal 2004, the Company sold,
net of its retained interest, $1,696&nbsp;million of trade receivables and remitted
to bank conduits, pursuant to a servicing agreement, $1,692&nbsp;million in
collections on those receivables. The net proceeds were used to reduce
borrowings under the Company&#146;s revolving credit facilities. The amount of
outstanding receivables under the agreement was $162.6&nbsp;million at March&nbsp;31,
2004 and $158.9&nbsp;million at March&nbsp;31, 2003.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The transaction has been accounted for as a sale under the provisions of
SFAS No.&nbsp;140<I>, Accounting for Transfers and Servicing of Financial Assets and
Extinguishments of Liabilities</I>. Under the securitization agreement, eligible
trade receivables are sold to bank conduits through a bankruptcy-remote special
purpose entity, which is consolidated for financial reporting purposes. The
difference between the proceeds from the sale and the carrying value of the
receivables is recognized as &#147;Discount on securitization of trade receivables&#148;
in the accompanying Consolidated Statements of Earnings and varies on a monthly
basis depending on the amount of receivables sold and market rates. The
Company retains a subordinated interest in the receivables sold, which is
recorded at the receivables&#146; previous carrying value. A subordinated retained
interest of approximately $44&nbsp;million and $45&nbsp;million is included in &#147;Trade
receivables&#148; in the accompanying Consolidated Balance Sheets at March&nbsp;31, 2004
and 2003, respectively. The Company&#146;s retained interest is generally collected
within 60&nbsp;days. On a monthly basis, management measures the fair value of the
retained interest at management&#146;s best estimate of the undiscounted expected
future cash collections on the transferred receivables. Changes
in the fair value are recognized as bad debt expense. Actual cash collections
may differ from these estimates and would directly affect the fair value of the
retained interest. In accordance with a servicing agreement, the Company
continues to service, administer and collect the trade receivables on behalf of
the bank conduits. The servicing fees charged to the bank conduits approximate
the costs of collections.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(14)&nbsp;STOCKHOLDERS&#146; EQUITY



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>(a)&nbsp;Common Stock</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company is authorized to issue up to 200&nbsp;million shares of common
stock with a par value of $0.01 per share. At March&nbsp;31, 2004, the number of
shares of common stock outstanding was 73.4&nbsp;million, excluding 1.5&nbsp;million
shares of common stock held as treasury stock (923 thousand shares owned by
National Welders), and 2.2&nbsp;million shares of common stock held in the Employee
Benefits Trust as described below. At March&nbsp;31, 2003, the number of shares of
common stock outstanding was 72.4&nbsp;million, excluding 547 thousand shares of
common stock held as treasury stock and 3.4&nbsp;million shares of common stock held
by the Employee Benefits Trust.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>(b)&nbsp;Preferred Stock and Redeemable Preferred Stock</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company is authorized to issue up to 20&nbsp;million shares of preferred
stock. Of the 20&nbsp;million shares authorized, 200 thousand shares have been
designated as Series&nbsp;A Junior Participating Preferred Stock and 200 thousand
shares have been designated as Series&nbsp;B Junior Participating Preferred Stock
(see <I>Stockholder Rights Plan </I>below). At March&nbsp;31, 2004 and 2003, no shares of
the preferred stock were issued or outstanding. The preferred stock may be
issued from time to time by the Company&#146;s Board of Directors in one or more
series. The Board of Directors is authorized to fix the dividend rights and
terms, conversion rights, voting rights, rights and terms of redemption,
liquidation preferences, and any other rights, preferences, privileges and
restrictions of any series of preferred stock, and the number of shares
constituting each such series and designation thereof.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Additionally, the Company is authorized to issue 30 thousand shares of
redeemable preferred stock. At March&nbsp;31, 2004 and 2003, no shares of
redeemable preferred stock were issued or outstanding.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">F-28
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">

<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>AIRGAS, INC. AND SUBSIDIARIES<BR>
NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS</B>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(14)&nbsp;STOCKHOLDERS&#146; EQUITY &#150; (Continued)



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(c) <I>Dividends</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;On May&nbsp;13, 2003, the Company&#146;s Board of Directors declared the first
quarterly cash dividend in the Company&#146;s history of $0.04 per share, which was
paid to stockholders on June&nbsp;30, 2003. Subsequently, at the end of each
quarter during fiscal 2004, the Company paid its stockholders regular quarterly
cash dividends of $0.04 per share. In addition, on May&nbsp;25, 2004, the Company
announced that its Board of Directors declared a regular quarterly cash
dividend of $0.045 per share, representing a 13% increase compared to the prior
year quarterly dividend. The dividend is payable June&nbsp;30, 2004 to stockholders
of record as of June&nbsp;15, 2004. Future dividend declarations and associated
amounts paid will depend upon the Company&#146;s earnings, financial condition, loan
covenants, capital requirements and other factors deemed relevant by management
and the Company&#146;s Board of Directors.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(d) <I>Shares in Employee Benefits Trust</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In March&nbsp;1999, the Company established a trust (&#147;the Employee Benefits
Trust&#148;) to fund certain future obligations of the Company&#146;s employee benefit
and compensation plans. From inception through fiscal 2001, the Company,
pursuant to a Common Stock Purchase Agreement, sold 7&nbsp;million shares of common
stock to the Employee Benefits Trust. The Company holds promissory notes from
the Employee Benefits Trust in the amount of each purchase. Shares held by the
Employee Benefits Trust serve as collateral for the promissory notes and are
available to fund certain employee benefit plan obligations as the promissory
notes are repaid. The shares held by the Employee Benefits Trust are not
considered outstanding for earnings per share purposes until they are released
from serving as collateral for the promissory notes. An independent
third-party financial institution serves as the Trustee. The Trustee votes or
tenders shares held by the trust in accordance with instructions received from
the participants in the employee benefit and compensation plans funded by the
trust. Approximately 1.2&nbsp;million and 909 thousand shares were issued from the
Employee Benefits Trust
for employee benefit programs during fiscal 2004 and 2003, respectively. As of
March&nbsp;31, 2004, the Employee Benefits Trust held 2.2&nbsp;million shares of the
Company&#146;s common stock.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(e) <I>Stockholder Rights Plan</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Effective April&nbsp;1, 1997, the Company&#146;s Board of Directors adopted a
stockholder rights plan (the &#147;1997 Rights Plan&#148;). Pursuant to the 1997 Rights
Plan, the Board of Directors declared a dividend distribution of one right for
each share of common stock. Each right entitles the holder to purchase from
the Company one one-thousandth of a share Series&nbsp;B Junior Participating
Preferred Stock at an initial exercise price of $100 per share.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Rights become exercisable only following the acquisition by a person or
group of 15% (or 20% in the case of the Chairman and certain of his affiliates)
or more of the Company&#146;s common stock or after the announcement of a tender
offer or exchange offer to acquire 15% (or 20% in the case of the Chairman and
certain of his affiliates) or more of the outstanding common stock. If such a
person or group acquires 15% or more (or 20% or more, as the case may be) of
the common stock, each right (other than such person&#146;s or group&#146;s rights, which
will become void) will entitle the holder to purchase, at the exercise price,
common stock having a market value equal to twice the exercise price. In
certain circumstances, the rights may be redeemed by the Company. If not
redeemed, they will expire on April&nbsp;1, 2007.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(f) <I>Warrants</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;During fiscal 2002, the Company granted warrants to purchase 324,000
shares of the Company&#146;s common stock to an outside consulting firm for services
rendered. The warrants have a term of three years from the date of grant and
have exercise prices in excess of market value on the date of grant ranging
from $11.98 to $18.78 per share. The aggregate value of the warrants on the
dates of grant, as determined by the Black-Scholes model, was $1.1&nbsp;million,
which the Company expensed during fiscal 2002. No additional warrants were granted
during fiscal years 2004 or 2003.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">F-29
</DIV>

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(15)&nbsp;STOCK-BASED COMPENSATION



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company has elected to continue to account for its stock-based
compensation plans in accordance with Accounting Principles Board Opinion No.
25, <I>Accounting for Stock Issued to Employees</I>, as permitted by SFAS No.&nbsp;123,
<I>Accounting for Stock-Based Compensation, </I>as amended by SFAS No.&nbsp;148.
Accordingly, no compensation expense has been recognized for its stock option
and employee stock purchase plans. The following table illustrates the effect
on net income and earnings per share for fiscal 2004, 2003 and 2002 as if the
Company had applied the fair value recognition provisions of SFAS 123 to
stock-based compensation.

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company&#146;s stock-based compensation plans are described below:


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt"><I>(a)&nbsp;Employee Stock Option Plans</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In May&nbsp;1997, the Company adopted the 1997 Stock Option Plan (the &#147;1997
Plan&#148;) under which officers and key employees may be granted options. The 1997
Plan replaced the previous 1984 Stock Option Plan. In May&nbsp;2002, the Company
amended the 1997 Plan to increase the total number of shares of common stock
reserved for sale upon the exercise of stock options and restricted stock
granted under the 1997 Plan from 8&nbsp;million to 11.2&nbsp;million. The amendment was
approved by the Company&#146;s stockholders in July&nbsp;2002.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Options granted under the 1997 Plan vest 25% annually and have a maximum
term of ten years. Under the 1997 Plan, at March&nbsp;31, 2004, 2003 and 2002, 3.8
million, 4.7&nbsp;million and 2.6&nbsp;million options, respectively, were available for
issuance. In fiscal 2004, 2003 and 2002, 1.1&nbsp;million, 1.2&nbsp;million and 1.5
million options, respectively, were granted with an exercise price equal to
market price at the date of grant. Options under the 1997 Plan are generally
granted in May of each year.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The fair value of each option grant is estimated on the date of grant
using the Black-Scholes option pricing model with the following weighted
average assumptions used for fiscal 2004, 2003 and 2002 option grants,
respectively: expected volatility of 40.1%, 40.3% and 48.8%, risk-free
interest rate of 2.6%, 4.3% and 4.7%, expected life of 7.3, 7.5, and 7.2&nbsp;years,
and a dividend yield of 0.83% (fiscal 2004). The weighted average fair value
per share of the options granted during fiscal 2004, 2003 and 2002 was $8.22,
$8.42 and $5.31, respectively.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">F-30
</DIV>

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Options for 4.7&nbsp;million, 5.1&nbsp;million and 4.7&nbsp;million shares were
exercisable at March&nbsp;31, 2004, 2003 and 2002, respectively.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(b) <I>Board of Directors Stock Option Plans</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In May&nbsp;1997, the Company adopted the 1997 Directors&#146; Stock Option Plan
(the &#147;1997 Directors&#146; Plan&#148;) under which directors of the Company who are not
employees may be granted options. The 1997 Directors&#146; Plan replaced the
previous 1989 Stock Option Plan for Directors. The 1997 Directors&#146; Plan
reserved 500 thousand shares for issuance. In May&nbsp;2004, the Company amended
the 1997 Directors&#146; Plan, subject to stockholder approval, to increase the
total number of shares of common stock reserved for issuance under the 1997
Directors&#146; Plan from 500 thousand to 800 thousand.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Under the 1997 Directors&#146; Plan, at March&nbsp;31, 2004, 49,500 options were
available for issuance. During fiscal 2004, 2003 and 2002, 60,000, 64,000 and
80,000 options, respectively, were granted with an exercise price equal to the
market price at the date of grant and have a maximum term of ten years.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The fair value of each option grant is estimated on the date of grant
using the Black-Scholes option pricing model with the following weighted
average assumptions used for fiscal 2004, 2003 and 2002 option grants,
respectively: expected volatility of 40.5%, 41.5% and 51.1%, risk-free interest
rate of 3.2%, 3.6% and 4.6%, expected life of 5.9, 5.9 and 5.7&nbsp;years, and a
dividend yield of 0.84% (fiscal 2004). The weighted average fair value per
share of the stock options granted during fiscal 2004, 2003 and 2002 was $7.58,
$6.70 and $6.61, respectively.


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">F-31
</DIV>

<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>AIRGAS, INC. AND SUBSIDIARIES<BR>
NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS</B>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(15)&nbsp;STOCK-BASED COMPENSATION &#150; (Continued)



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(c) <I>Employee Stock Purchase Plans</I>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In July&nbsp;2003, the Company&#146;s stockholders approved the 2003 Employee Stock
Purchase Plan (the &#147;2003 Plan&#148;) to encourage and assist employees in acquiring
an equity interest in the Company. The 2003 Plan replaced the Company&#146;s 2001
Employee Stock Purchase Plan (the &#147;2001 Plan&#148;). During fiscal 2004,
substantially all of the authorized shares remaining under the 2001 Plan were
issued. During fiscal 2005, the 2001 Plan will be terminated and the remaining
authorized shares will be deregistered.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The 2003 Plan is authorized to issue up to 1.5&nbsp;million shares of common
stock. Eligible employees may elect to have up to 15% of their annual gross
earnings withheld to purchase common stock at 85% of the market value. Market
value under the 2003 Plan is defined as either the closing share price on the
New York Stock Exchange as of an employee&#146;s enrollment date or the closing
price on the first business day of a fiscal quarter when the shares are
purchased, whichever is lower. An employee may lock-in a purchase price for up
to 12&nbsp;months. The 2003 Plan is designed to comply with the requirements of
Sections&nbsp;421 and 423 of the Internal Revenue Code. The terms of the Company&#146;s
previous 2001 Plan were substantially the same as the 2003 Plan, except that
under the 2001 Plan, employees could lock-in a purchase price for up to 27
months. During fiscal 2004 and 2003, the Company issued 554 thousand shares
and 809 thousand shares, provided from the Employee Benefits Trust, to fund
purchases made by employees under the 2003 and 2001 Plans. The average
purchase price per share in fiscal 2004 and 2003 was $12.44 and $11.06,
respectively. During fiscal 2002, the Company also issued 1.1&nbsp;million shares,
provided from the Employee Benefits Trust, to fund purchases made by employees
at an average price of $6.58 per share.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Compensation expense under SFAS 123 is measured based on the fair value of
the employees&#146; option to purchase shares of common stock at the grant date and
is recognized over the future periods in which the related employee service is
rendered. The fair value of the employees&#146; option to purchase shares of common
stock is estimated using the Black-Scholes model. The assumptions used to
estimate the fair value of the employees&#146; option to purchase shares of common
stock at grant dates in fiscal 2004 and 2002, respectively, were: average
expected volatility of 31.1% and 61%, average risk-free interest rate of 0.96%
and 2.6%, dividend yield of 0.87% (fiscal 2004), and expected option terms ranging
from 3 to 6&nbsp;months for the 2003 Plan and 3 to 27&nbsp;months for previous employee
stock purchase plans. Had the Company adopted SFAS 123, compensation expense
related to the Employee Stock Purchase Plans would have been $1.5&nbsp;million, $3.6
million and $4.1&nbsp;million in fiscal 2004, 2003 and 2002, respectively. Fiscal
2003 expense is related to the fiscal 2002 grant and represents the value of
the options as they vest over the employee service period.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(16)&nbsp;CONSOLIDATED AND UNCONSOLIDATED AFFILIATES



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;With the adoption of FIN 46R (Note 2), the Company&#146;s previously
unconsolidated joint venture affiliate, National Welders, became a
consolidated affiliate. The Company&#146;s other joint venture affiliates were
not impacted by FIN 46R. The Company has participated in a joint venture with
National Welders since June&nbsp;1996. National Welders is a producer and
distributor of industrial gases based in Charlotte, North Carolina. National
Welders owns and operates 46 branch stores, two acetylene plants, a specialty
gas lab, and three air separation plants that produce all of the joint
venture&#146;s oxygen and nitrogen and over 50% of its argon requirements. The
joint venture also distributes medical and specialty gases, processed
chemicals and welding equipment and supplies.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Ownership interests in National Welders consist of voting common stock
and voting redeemable preferred stock with a 5% annual dividend. The Company
owns 100% of the joint venture&#146;s common stock, which represents a 50% voting
interest. A family holds approximately 3.2&nbsp;million shares of redeemable
preferred stock and controls the balance of the voting interest. Between
June&nbsp;30, 2006 and June&nbsp;30, 2009, the preferred stockholders have the option
to redeem their preferred shares for cash at a price of $17.78 per share or to
tender them to the joint venture in exchange



<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt">F-33
</DIV>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#tocpage">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>
<DIV style="font-family: 'Times New Roman',Times,serif">


<P align="center" style="font-size: 10pt"><B>AIRGAS, INC. AND SUBSIDIARIES<BR>
NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS</B>



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">(16) CONSOLIDATED AND UNCONSOLIDATED AFFILIATES &#150; (Continued)



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">for approximately 2.3&nbsp;million shares of Airgas common stock. If Airgas&#146;
common stock has a market value of $24.45 per share, the common stock and
cash redemption options are equivalent. If the preferred stockholders elect
to exchange their shares for Airgas common stock, the Company is obligated to
provide the necessary shares to the joint venture by capital contribution or
other means the Company reasonably deems appropriate. The Company may
purchase shares on the open market or may issue new or treasury shares to
meet its exchange obligation. The preferred stockholders may also elect to
retain their interest in the preferred stock beyond June&nbsp;30, 2009.



<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;FIN 46R addresses the consolidation of variable interest entities.
Variable interest entities are defined as corporations, partnerships, trusts,
or any other legal structure used for business purposes, and by design, the
holders of equity instruments in those entities lack one of the
characteristics of a financial controlling interest. FIN 46R changes
previous accounting practice by introducing the concept of a &#147;Primary
Beneficiary&#148; and requiring variable interest entities to be consolidated by
the party deemed to be the Primary Beneficiary (i.e., the party that is
subject to a majority of the risk of loss from the variable interest entity&#146;s
activities or entitled to receive a majority of the entity&#146;s residual returns
or both).


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company determined that National Welders met the definition of a
&#147;Variable Interest Entity&#148; under FIN 46R. Additionally, the Company, as the
only common stockholder, was determined to be the Primary Beneficiary of the
joint venture. Therefore, effective December&nbsp;31, 2003, the Company elected
to adopt FIN 46R, as it applies to the joint venture, and consolidated
National Welders. As permitted by FIN 46R, the Company applied the
interpretation prospectively from the date of adoption (prior periods not restated). Therefore, at
December&nbsp;31, 2003, the consolidation of National Welders only affected the
balance sheet. There was no cumulative effect adjustment as a result of the
consolidation. The consolidation had the effect of eliminating the Company&#146;s
$62&nbsp;million investment in National Welders and recording the joint venture&#146;s
assets, liabilities and a corresponding minority interest liability. The
minority interest liability represents the redemption value of the joint
venture&#146;s preferred stock ($57&nbsp;million), net of a $21&nbsp;million note
receivable, bearing interest at 8%, due from the preferred stockholders. At
March&nbsp;31, 2004, National Welders&#146; impact on the consolidated balance sheet is
summarized below:

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The assets and liabilities of National Welders at March&nbsp;31, 2004
include $55.1&nbsp;million of goodwill and $53.8&nbsp;million of debt, which is
non-recourse to the Company.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company recognized $4.4&nbsp;million of &#147;Equity in Earnings of
Unconsolidated Affiliates&#148; related to National Welders through the
nine-months ended December&nbsp;31, 2003. With the prospective adoption
permitted by FIN 46R, the Company&#146;s proportionate share of the joint
venture&#146;s operating results for the fourth fiscal quarter was not reflected
as &#147;Equity in Earnings of Unconsolidated Affiliates.&#148; Rather, the fourth
fiscal quarter operating results of the joint venture were reflected broadly
across the income statement with minority interest expense reflecting the
quarterly dividend on the joint venture&#146;s redeemable preferred stock, net of
interest earned on the note receivable from the preferred stockholders. For
both the fourth quarter and fiscal year 2004, National Welders contributed

insurance gain of $1.7&nbsp;million.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company accounts for investments in unconsolidated affiliates by the
equity method of accounting, as described in Note 1(b). The Company&#146;s
investment in its unconsolidated affiliates totaled approximately $6.3&nbsp;million
and $66&nbsp;million at March&nbsp;31, 2004 and 2003, respectively. Unconsolidated
affiliates in Fiscal 2003 included a $59&nbsp;million investment in National
Welders, which was consolidated in Fiscal 2004. Unconsolidated affiliates at
March&nbsp;31, 2004 principally consist of a 50% joint venture interest in AC
Industries, which is a carbon dioxide production plant reported under All Other
Operations in Note 24. The Company&#146;s share of earnings from AC Industries was
$830 thousand; $1.1&nbsp;million and $1.0&nbsp;million for the years ended March&nbsp;31,
2004, 2003, and 2002, respectively.

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Pursuant to an accelerated depreciation provision of a fiscal 2002 change
in the tax law, the Company anticipated receiving a tax refund of approximately
$19&nbsp;million during fiscal 2003 related to the assets acquired in the Air
Products acquisition. After further review, it was concluded that the Company
would not be eligible for the refund in fiscal 2003, but instead would receive
the cash benefit over the next four years. As a result, an adjustment was made
in the first quarter of fiscal 2003 that resulted in approximately a $19
million reclassification between current and deferred income taxes. The
adjustment did not impact net earnings or operating cash flows.

<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;The Company has recorded tax benefits amounting to $6.2&nbsp;million, $5.8
million, and $4.3&nbsp;million in fiscal 2004, 2003 and 2002, respectively,
resulting from the exercise of stock options. This benefit has been recorded in
capital in excess of par value.


<P align="left" style="font-size: 10pt">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;In assessing the realizability of deferred tax assets, management
considers whether it is more likely than not that some portion or all of the
deferred tax assets will not be realized. The ultimate realization of deferred
tax assets is dependent upon the generation of future taxable income during the
periods in which those temporary differences become deductible. Management
considers the reversal of deferred tax liabilities and projected future taxable
income in making this assessment. Based upon the level of historical taxable
income and projections for future taxable income over the periods which the
deferred tax assets are deductible, management believes it is more likely than
not that the Company will realize the benefits of these deductible differences,
net of the existing valuation allowances, at March&nbsp;31, 2004. Valuation
allowances primarily relate to certain state tax net operating loss
carryforwards.
